chapters.summary: Council staff welcomes attendees to the Committee on Children and Youth hearing, asks everyone to silence devices, and instructs those wishing to speak to fill out appearance cards.
chapters.title: Council staff opens hearing and announces housekeeping rules

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Welcome to today's New York City Council hearing for the Committee on Children and Youth.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, please silence all phones and electronic devices.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If you wish to speak in today's hearing, please fill out an appearance card with one of the sergeants.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Moving forward, no one is to approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair, you may begin.

chapters.summary: Council Member Althea Stevens introduces the hearing on DYCD's Beacon and Cornerstone programs and lists the resolutions being heard, including sports betting revenue for youth sports, the Center for Fatherhood Initiatives, baby bond pilot programs, food advertising regulation, and guaranteed income programs.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens introduces oversight topic and agenda resolutions

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Good afternoon and welcome to today's hearing on children and youth on the Children and Youth Committee.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Althea Stevens, Chair of the Committee.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you for joining us here today where this committee will be evaluating DYCD's Beacons and Cornerstones program.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: In addition to the oversight topic, we'll be also hearing the following resolutions.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Reso 32, sponsored by Councilmember Banks, setting aside a portion of state revenue from state betting to support youth.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Sports programs upgrade community sports infrastructure and expand access to recreational activities for young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Reso 142, sponsored by Councilman Riley, calling for the establishment of the Center for Fatherhood Initiative under the Office of Children and Family Services.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Reso 161, Deputy Speaker Williams calling for the establishment of Baby Bond Pilot Program for foster youth.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Reso 217, sponsored by Councilmember Hudson, calling for the regulation of false or misleading advertising related to food products to protect children.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Reso 276, sponsored by Deputy William Speaker, which will create a targeted, meaningful test guarantee income program for low-income individuals, young adults, and foster youth.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Reso 277 by Deputy...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Speaker Williams calling for the creation of a federally funded savings account for every child and Reso 278 also sponsored by Deputy

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Speaker Williams calling for the management of funds and eligible child care bank accounts by the state controller.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Those are all the resos we're hearing so now we'll actually get to what we're really here for.

chapters.summary: Stevens describes Beacon and Cornerstone centers as trusted neighborhood institutions serving tens of thousands of New Yorkers, highlights their role in combating youth loneliness and anxiety, and notes the last comprehensive procurements were in 2017 and 2015.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens outlines the importance of Beacon and Cornerstone programs

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: For decades, Beacon and Cornerstone Centers have served as trusted neighborhood institutions.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They provide young people and adults with safe spaces to learn, build relationships, develop skills, access support services, and engage with their communities.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Could I have some tissue?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Today, there are 92 beacon programs and 100 cornerstones programs operating throughout the city that serves tens of thousands of New Yorkers each year.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: This number reflects the significant demand for accessible community-based programming and critical roles that those centers play.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Critical role that those centers play.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Those programs are particularly important at a time when many young people continue to reflect significant challenges.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: As young people are experiencing extraordinary high rates of loneliness, anxiety, and social isolation, research consistently

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: demonstrates that participation in structural community-based programs improve educational outcomes, strengthen social connections, support mental health, and promotes positive youth development.

chapters.summary: Stevens discusses provider concerns about funding levels, staffing challenges, security costs, and facility conditions, noting providers absorb costs beyond contract awards. She calls for evaluating DYCD's future procurement plans and program expansion.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens on provider concerns and the need for future procurement

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: At the same time, today's hearing provides an opportunity to assess whether our investments have kept the pace with the expectations we place on these programs.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The last comprehensive beacon procurement was issued in 2017, and the last cornerstone procurement was issued in 2015.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Since then, providers have continued to deliver services through contract renewals and extension, but providers have consistently raised concerns regarding funding levels, staffing challenges, security costs, and conditions of the facilities.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Many providers report that they are absorbing substantial costs beyond their contract awards to continue operating these vital programs.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: As we look at the future, it is important that we evaluate whether providers have the resources necessary to meet the needs of the communities they serve.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: We look forward to discussing DYC's plan for future procurement, potential programming, expansion, and opportunities to strengthen community-based programs citywide.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Their testimony...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And testimony from community members will help inform

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How Citi can continue to support these critical community institutions.

chapters.summary: Stevens reads a statement from Council Member Nantasha Williams on Resolutions 161, 276, 277, and 278, addressing the racial wealth gap, baby bond programs for foster youth, federally funded savings accounts, and guaranteed income programs.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens reads statement from Council Member Williams on wealth inequality resolutions

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Before we begin, I would like to thank committee staff, including Senior Policy Analyst Elizabeth Arts, our Senior Committee Counsel,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Real.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Agassiz for their hard work in preparing for this hearing, as well as my wonderful staff back at District 16.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I will now turn it over to the committee council to swear in the administration.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Oh, I have another statement I have to read.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It's very long.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: This is from Dr.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Dantasha Williams, Council Member Dr.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Dantasha Williams.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Committee on Children and Youth Hearing statement on Reso 161-276-277-278.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Wealthy inequalities have often been described as one of the defining process issues of our time.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: However, while the wealth gap in our country and our city has reached alarming levels, we know those disproportionate are the result of deep-rooted historical and systematic discrimination against

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: some of the most marginalized community, particularly black and brown New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Still today, the medium household net worth for black families and 14 times lower than that of white families in New York.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: This reality, again, is underscored by state, city, state, and federal government have long known.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And at a time,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: failed aquatic at a time has failed to add a

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: We address those who've been historically disadvantaged have also been systematically denied meaningful opportunities to build financial security for themselves and future generations.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Despite longstanding barriers that continue to particularly circle.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The cycle of poverty, lawmakers are every at every level of government introduced legislation aimed to narrowing the wealth gap.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: One proposal that has gained significant attention is this creation of a so-called baby bond program.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Those programs establish government funded and managed savings accounts for newborns, helping create long term financial assets that can be

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: assess in adulthood, access in adulthood.

chapters.summary: Stevens continues reading Williams's statement, detailing Resolution 161's call for a foster youth baby bond pilot, Resolution 277's American Opportunity Accounts Act, and Resolution 278's state-managed savings accounts, citing research on the long-term impact of modest savings.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens continues reading Williams statement on baby bonds and savings accounts

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Reso 161 would call the New York State legislation to pass Assembly Bill A7415, sponsored by Assemblymember Khalil Anderson, which would establish a BB bond pilot program for foster youth who

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: disproportionately face financial instability.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: In adulthood.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Reso 277 will call on Congress to pass S441 slash HR1041, also known as the American Opportunity Account Act, sponsored by Senator Cory Booker

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: and Congresswoman Presley.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Reso 278 will call on New York State to pass Senate Bill S4564 and Assembly Bill A60.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: 5-9 introduced by Senator Jamal Bailey and Assemblymember Michael Benedetto.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: These will require the state to deposit a $1,000 allocation into accounts managed by the controller.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Additionally, Resolution 278 will call on Congress to pass legislation creating a meaningful, tested, guaranteed income program for low-income individuals, young adults, and foster care youth.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Research also shows that even small amounts of savings could have meaningful long-term impacts, including reducing the future state loan debt.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Lowering chronic financial stress, and increasing household wealth, with particularly significant gains for black and Latino families.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Those findings make it clear that even modest investments in financial security can create meaningful generational changes.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: We must continue to advance serious conversation and bold policies that help close the wealth gap and expand opportunities for our young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Stevens acknowledges Council Members Felder and Brewer, joking about Brewer's seriousness on community centers, and then turns the floor over to committee counsel to swear in the administration panel.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens acknowledges council members and prepares for administration testimony

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I would like to acknowledge Councilmember Felder and Councilmember Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Oh, thank you for being here, Councilmember Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Only the OGs are here.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: We're so blessed.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And let me tell you guys, ZYCD, get ready, because Councilmember Brewer is serious about these community centers.

chapters.summary: Council staff administers the truth affirmation to the DYCD administration panelists before they begin testimony.
chapters.title: Council staff administers affirmation to administration panelists

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: We will now hear testimony from the administration.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Before we begin, I will administer the affirmation.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Panelists, please raise your right hand.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I will read the affirmation once, then call on each of you individually to respond.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee, and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Wanda Asherall, Associate Commissioner of DYCD, introduces herself and colleague Threcia Harris, and provides an overview of the 92 Beacon and 100 Cornerstone programs, describing their year-round operations and role as community hubs.
chapters.title: DYCD Associate Commissioner Wanda Asherall introduces Beacon and Cornerstone overview

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Okay, that's close.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Let me know if you, oh, I can hear myself.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Stevens and members.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I think it's just this one.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Stevens and members of the Children and Youth Committee.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Bring it closer.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: No, no worries.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Let me know if it's too loud.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Stevens and members of the Children and Youth Committee.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I am Wanda Asherall, Associate Commissioner of the New York City Department of Youth and Community Development.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I am joined by Threcia Harris, Assistant Commissioner for Community Centers.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to discuss DYCD's Beacon Cornerstone Community Centers.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: DYCD's community centers include 92 beacon programs located in public schools across New York City and 100 cornerstone programs operating in New York City housing authority developments.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: They operate year-round in non-school hours, during non-school hours, on weekdays, weekends, and in the summer.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Each center transforms its host location into a resource for the whole community by offering an integrated range of programming tailored to local needs.

chapters.summary: Asherall details FY25 enrollment figures—62,000 youth and 17,000 adults in Beacons, 21,000 youth and 6,700 adults in Cornerstones—and describes DYCD's monitoring system, site visits, and corrective action plans.
chapters.title: Asherall on program reach, enrollment numbers, and monitoring systems

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: For all participants, especially youth, community centers offer a safe place to engage.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: engage in recreational activities, discover new interests, acquire skills, and find opportunities to contribute to the community, guided and supported by program staff.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Centers provide activities and services for school-age youth, families, and adults ages 22 years and older.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Including seniors.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: In keeping with the original concept, programs make special efforts to engage hard-to-recruit youth and young adults for whom participation in activities that foster positive youth development,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: social-emotional learning, SEL, and leadership skills are especially beneficial.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: DYCD community centers have a broad reach.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: In fiscal year 25, Beacon programs served more than 62,000 youth and 17,000 adults.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Cornerstone programs range in size from smaller centers to large, multi-purpose community spaces.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: In fiscal year 25, Cornerstone Community Centers served over 21,000 young people and 6,700 adults.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: DYCD monitors program performance and provides technical assistance to help programs reach their contract and community goals.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: DYCD program managers make periodic on-site visits to programs to ensure that sites are welcoming,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: properly staffed, engaging the community, and operating at full capacity.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: DYCD program managers also monitor

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: whether enrollment and attendance are consistent with contracted levels and program goals.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: DYCD's evaluation and monitoring system in DYCD Connect documents detailed program evaluations in four key sections, program practice, program compliance,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: administration, and outcomes and outputs.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: If a program does not meet the established standards, DYCD may issue a strategic action plan or a corrective action plan and provides on-site coaching and capacity building resources if there are issues or indicators that need

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: serious and formal correction.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: This includes but is not limited to contract compliance, program quality, and outcomes and safety violations.

chapters.summary: Asherall describes the Saturday Night Lights sports program with 141 sites, and a mental health pilot initiative partnering with the Mayor's Office and the New School to implement the EASE intervention for youth anxiety and depression at Beacon and Cornerstone centers.
chapters.title: Asherall on Saturday Night Lights and mental health support initiatives

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Beacon and Cornerstone Community Centers host specialized activities and participate in various citywide initiatives to support and enhance offerings to the community.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: These include Saturday Night Lights, which is a sports program

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: that has enhanced community center programming by offering young people year-round free sports and enrichment activities on Saturday evenings.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: The SNL program provides basketball, soccer, dance, and other sports and arts services in the community centers.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And schools, most of which are co-located with Beacon and Cornerstone programs.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: There are

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: 141 SNL programs throughout the city, of which 46 are located in Beacon and 50 are located in Cornerstone Community Centers.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: In fiscal year 25, SNL programs served 4,424 participants.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We also offer mental health support services.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: DYCD participated in a pilot initiative in partnership with the Mayor's Office of Community Mental Health to introduce new skills for program staff on mental health coping strategies they can use to work with young people.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: In partnership with the New School Center for Global Mental Health, DYCD facilitated the first adaptation and implementation of early adolescent skills for emotions, referred to EASE, in the United States.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: The intervention was specifically developed to provide a scalable, culturally adaptable, and preventive model of care for youth experiencing symptoms of anxiety,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: stress, depression, and other forms of psychological distress.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: The EASE initiative focused on Beacon and Cornerstone Community Centers by training center staff to support children in identifying and understanding their emotions, building on their strengths, and practicing a host of social and emotional learning skills.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: EASE also supported caregivers in practicing active listening, positive discipline, spending quality time with their children, and

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Practicing self-care.

chapters.summary: Asherall highlights the 'My New York Story' intergenerational program pairing Cornerstone sites with older adult centers, and the Step It Up NYC youth dance and social impact initiative, which held its 2026 championship at the United Palace.
chapters.title: Asherall on intergenerational storytelling and Step It Up programs

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: My New York Story is an intergenerational storytelling initiative developed in partnership with the New York City Department of Aging that brings together older adults and young people to build meaningful

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: relationships through shared experiences, dialogue, and creative expression.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Over the course of a six-week program, participants engaged in guided storytelling workshops that encouraged them to explore themes of identity, resilience, community, and belonging while learning from one another's lived experiences.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: The program culminated in a citywide celebration where young people and older adults showcased their stories and reflected on the connections they formed across generations.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: In 2026, the program paired four DYCD cornerstone sites with neighboring older adult centers, including Eastchester Community Center, Cypress Hills,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Pink Community Center, and Grand Street Settlement Roosevelt Community Center.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Through weekly workshops, participants strengthened intergenerational understanding, developed communication and leadership, and created a shared space where personal histories could be preserved and celebrated.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: The culminating event highlighted the power of these connections and demonstrated how intergenerational programming can foster empathy, civic engagement, and foster communities across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Step it up.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Our annual Step It Up program was a huge success.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Step It Up NYC is the Y City's premier youth programming arts and social impact initiative, empowering young people to use dance,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: storytelling, and creative expression as tools for advocacy and community change.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: More than...

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: More than a competition, Step It Up NYC challenges youth teams to develop original performances and accompany social impact campaigns that address issues affecting their communities.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: On June 4th, 2026, 10 finalist teams

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: representing the YCD-funded Beacon, Cornerstone, and Compass programs across New York City took stage at the historic United Palace before a crowd of 1,100 attendees to raise awareness about critical topics including

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: teen violence,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: domestic violence, incarceration, peer pressure, discrimination, and cyberbullying.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Through months of preparation, participants strengthened leadership, teamwork, public speaking, and civic engagement skills while amplifying youth voices on issues that matter most to them.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: The 2026 Step It Up Championship was awarded to two Beacon and two Cornerstone Community Centers.

chapters.summary: Asherall notes providers are preparing for summer camp, Summer Rising, and expanded summer hours at Cornerstone centers, and expresses readiness to answer questions from the committee.
chapters.title: Asherall concludes with summer programming preparations

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: DYCD and community center providers are preparing busily for summer programming.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: which will include summer camp, summer rising, and expanded summer programming hours in Cornstone Community Centers.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Thank you again for the opportunity to discuss DYCD's community centers and my favorite topic.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We look forward to answering your questions.

chapters.summary: Stevens presses on why there has been no new Beacon RFP since 2017 and asks for the agency's timeline for releasing a concept paper and RFP. Asherall says the agency is committed to releasing a concept paper and is examining funding, staffing, and program model needs.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions DYCD on timeline for new Beacon RFP

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Well, it's not only my favorite topic.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They have a special place in my heart because if you've come to the many hearings that I've had, I've always talked about I spent a lot of time running community centers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I just feel like we did all the work.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But, you know, I thought that was the best place.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But let's jump into some of these questions.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: DYCD hasn't had a new beacon RFP since 2017.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What is the agency's timeline for leasing a new concept paper and RFP for Beacon Community Centers?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And what factors contributed to the delay?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I also want to put out there, we're still in the middle of the after school RFP.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I get it if you're like, girl, I don't want to hear RFP.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But I got to ask the questions because this is important because that is a long timeline.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I think that part of the issue and confusion around this RFP is because they have them so long.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: People are like, wait, I don't want change.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so not having RFPs doesn't work either.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I really would love to hear what's...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Is there a plan?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Are there talks?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Is that in the work?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And trying to figure out where we're at with that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because beacons is one of the things I actually love because

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: When you think about we can't build, we don't have that many community centers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So this allows us to activate the schools in our community to now be community centers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I think that in spaces that don't have community centers, we should be looking at using more schools.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I remember the last mayor, we had talked about that, but that never came to fruition.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Whatever.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I appreciate the question, and beacons are dear to my heart.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I've been working with beacons for over 20 years, so definitely dear to my heart.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Beacon community centers are incredibly important to the neighborhoods they serve.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: They operate, as you know, as a true community hub, serving young people and families.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And you've seen the increase in young people engagement in the PMMR and the MMR and adult engagement.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: That's a testament to the need of these services in this community.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so, you know, our commissioner, and she's testified before, but we are committed to releasing a concept paper at some point to really, you know, realign the model.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we want to be intentional about what the services are, you know, the model itself.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And we're going to take deeper dives on that.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We're going to really look at the funding.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And be intentional about what is the funding, what is the true cost of operating.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: a robust, holistic community center that not just only serves young people, but we're serving adults and we're serving seniors and we're serving the larger community.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we really want to look at what does it take to operate?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: What are the staffing needs?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: What is that structure?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And we are looking across all of our...

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Our entire footprint of DYCD, fatherhood, workforce.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We're looking at all of those different pieces and really looking at the funding and figuring out what does that look like in a community center.

chapters.summary: Stevens questions why SNL operates as a standalone program rather than being integrated into Beacon and Cornerstone models. Asherall says DYCD is exploring whether to fold SNL into a future concept paper and RFP.
chapters.title: Stevens and Asherall discuss integrating Saturday Night Lights into program model

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, I think a lot of the work that's being done needs to be connected.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: In your testimony, you spoke about one of the programs that I don't like as much, which is Saturday Night Lights programs, which is SNL.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I've never been shy to explain my thanks with that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And you explained that SNL has been in both the beacons and in the cornerstones.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And one of the reasons why I don't like it is because it's a standalone program and it doesn't make sense.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so my question is to you guys, are you guys looking to roll that just into the model moving forward?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And why wasn't that done originally to not just have that become part of the program model when you did one of these contract extensions or something like that?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It's on.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The green means it's on.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So turn it back.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I can't speak to how the timing and how it all rolled out with the SNL, but what I can say is this, is that we're not planning

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: to have an RFP for the SNL, but we are looking at ways to realign it and think about whether or not, to your point, it makes most sense to integrate it into a future concept paper and an RFP.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So that's the exercise that we are.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Are doing internally to discuss, like, how does this live within the fabric of our community centers?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I mean, again, like, the program in itself, like, it makes sense, but it does not make sense to me as a standalone.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I think that it should be a part of the program, right?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I think some of the, you know, it started out as a separate program.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And what happens is people hear things when it's good, then everybody wants it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so I think that it was one of those moments we should have stepped back and been like, wait a minute, is this something we could just expand throughout all of the portfolio opposed to having the touch points in the way we do.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I'm happy to hear that that's something that's being considered as we're thinking about moving into a concept paper.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about the 76 standalone Beacon programs and 15 ACS-linked Beacons from the 2017 RFP. Asherall confirms the ACS preventive piece has been moved under ACS's direct funding and monitoring, leaving the Beacons separate.
chapters.title: Stevens questions status of 2017 Beacon RFP's ACS preventive component

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: In 2017, DYCD issued two beacon RFPs.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The first RFP was for 76 beacon standalone programs at DOE.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The second was for 15 with ACS programs, which provided community-based programs.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: programs families with youth at risk of foster care placement.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Those RFP were set to expire in 2020 with the option for renewal for additional three years.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Given that this is now six years past the initial end date, can you please give me an update on whether

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Those 76 beacon standalone programs and 15 beacons with ACS programs are still available.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What's the level of funding available for them and whether there have been adjustments that have or will be made to those programs?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So when we issued the RFP for Beacon back in 2017, yes, we did have an ACS preventive component.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Since then, Chair Stevens, we no longer have the ACS preventive piece attached to a cornerstone.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We've since...

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Moved those services, those services supervision under ACS.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Not ACS, is it ACS?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yeah, ACS.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so they are solely funding and monitoring those programs directly.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so are they still, was the ACS piece just pulled out and the beacons were left?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yes, that is the case.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Well, I was looking so shocked because my brother in government, Assemblymember Landon Diaz, just walked in the room.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So look at that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Look at that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The state visiting the city.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Love that.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks how Beacon contracts have changed to account for inflation and rising costs since 2017. Asherall explains DYCD has honored approved indirect cost rates and is now examining true operating costs for future funding allocations.
chapters.title: Stevens questions Beacon contract budget changes and inflation

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Since 2017 procurement, how have Beacon's contracts budget changed from accounting for inflation wages and raising operation costs?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So we have, over the years we've worked with the indirect costs have been wages and indirect expenses have been allocated to the budgets.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: For non-federally funded ICR rates, DYCD has honored the city's approved ICR rate.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: For any of the contracts.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And then for federally funded programs, we've complied with the regulations and negotiated indirect cost rates if they exist.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we've been able to do, you know,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: address those pieces in that way.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: But again, we are looking, it is a 10-year-old contract and budget, and so we are intentionally looking at the funding allocations and the true cost of operating a program.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we hope to continue those conversations and have stakeholder engagement, work with our oversight organizations, OMB, City Hall, Council, to help inform

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: what the allocation should look like.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about challenges in maintaining partnerships between Beacon providers and host schools. Asherall describes coordination at central and ground levels, including space allocation, extended use expenses, and required safety meetings with principals.
chapters.title: Stevens questions DYCD on Beacon-DOE school partnership challenges

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because beacon programs operate within the public school, strong coordination with school leadership is essential.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What challenges DYCD has identified in maintaining partnerships between beacon providers and host schools, and what supports does those agencies provide when conflict arise regarding space scheduling and program operation?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I mean, I think especially with the beacons, they've been around for so long.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: A lot of those partnerships are like.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: 20 years in at this point.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I think it looks a lot different, but I guess, can you talk a little bit about what those challenges still might be that you're having?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because I remember when I was in school and there was a beacon, there used to be a lot of issues around the opening of the building and who's going to be there and all the things.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yeah, I will say that beacons are part of the fabric of the school and the community, and they serve as a critical hub.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so making resources available to the families and making sure that it's quality services is important.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We work very closely with the Department of Education, both at the central level but also at the ground level.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so my team, there's two tiers, right?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: On the ground level, our CBO partners work very closely with the principals to have access to space.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: The schools allocate space.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: The beacons have access to office location.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And then we work closely with the Department of Education where we cover the expenses for extended use, including evening use of the space.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We work with our CBOs on ensuring that they have a standing meeting with their principals to go over school safety.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I think that at the end of the day, we want to make sure that young people and the participants and the staff are safe.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so they are required to work closely with the school on safety.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And putting systems in place.

chapters.summary: Stevens raises provider concerns about restricted community access to school buildings. Asherall calls it 'a dance' and says principals increasingly see Beacons as valuable for family engagement, leading to more collaboration.
chapters.title: Stevens questions community access to DOE buildings outside school hours

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Providers have raised concerns that the school administration restricts community access to DOE buildings outside of school hours.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How does DYCD ensure that beacon sites remain accessible to community members outside of school hours?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Has the agency received complaints regarding restricted access to the DOE facility?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: It's a dance.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: It's a dance.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I think that principals and school administrators and New York City Public School in general know the value and the importance of community engagement.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And I think that one of the things that they've highlighted before is that our beacon programs are really good at engaging families and engaging them.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: larger communities.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so the principals are now figuring out how do I leverage that resource and that opportunity to expand my reach, their reach of families and community members.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we are seeing an uptake in more collaboration and engagement and dialogue to ensure that

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: the families outside of the school are being served.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we support the schools, we support the CBOs in making sure that those things are in place.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I think it's always a dance, but again, I know just because beacons have been around for so long, they have like a very different relationship.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: As well.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks how DYCD evaluates the quality of required community partnerships and advisory councils. Asherall explains providers leverage co-locators and community partners to bring in resources at no additional cost, with DYCD facilitating connections.
chapters.title: Stevens questions evaluation of Beacon community partnerships and advisory councils

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The RFE requires that each beacon program to maintain at least three community partnerships and operate advisory council.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How does the YCD evaluate the quality and effectiveness of those partnerships and are providers receiving significant resources to support that community work and engagement?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So all of our provider in the contract, yes, they have partners, but they also have co-locators.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so the idea is to be able to leverage those resources so that it's not an additional cost to them.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So if there's a co-locator able to provide dance and they're unable to do so, that co-locator can expand their services and their reach to the participants of the beacon as well as clients that they're bringing in.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: into the school.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so the idea is to ensure that our CBOs, you know, reach out to their local organizations and communities to bring in those resources.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And how we support them is that, you know, sometimes we send them resources.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Sometimes we'll have somebody reach out to us directly.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And oftentimes we'll say,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: hey, we have a potential partner that provides services in your community.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Can you meet with them?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And it's usually a win-win conversation, and the incoming smaller organization is then able to use the space at no cost to them.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we do it in that way where they can kind of partner on the ground.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about feedback on the 2017 RFP's requirement for dedicated outreach coordinators to reach chronically absent youth. Asherall reports positive feedback, noting coordinators connect with local partners and hard-to-reach youth, with DYCD providing targeted technical assistance.
chapters.title: Stevens questions effectiveness of Beacon outreach to chronically absent youth

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The RFP in 2017 required Beacon to conduct targeted outreach to reach chronically absent youth and encourage providers to employ dedicated outreach coordinators.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What feedback has DYCD collected on the effectiveness of those outreach requirements?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We've received positive feedback as far as having dedicated personnel to do outreach.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: It allows the program to have that individual meet with local NDAs, meet with

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: local potential partners, the co-locators, so that it will alleviate the work of a director and or assistant director.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so this dedicated person is able to bring in and make connections.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: to hard to reach young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so how do you guys use that work, I guess, to me also thinking about to educate not only just speaking programs, but across the spectrum, right?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because, you know, there's so many programs that are working with young people.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yeah, we've over the years, well, that's one thing that we definitely want to take a deeper dive because that was part of the 2017.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So as we're looking and unpacking all the different components of the current model, that is going to be one area that we're going to take a deeper dive on and see what's been working, what has not been working, and the effectiveness, to your point.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: However, we have been able to strategically...

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Target those outreach coordinators to provide them with technical assistance for themselves.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: How do you do outreach?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: How do you engage a young person?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: How do you engage families?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we've hosted meetings specifically for the outreach coordinators, and we've also made intentional connections on the ground with

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: our local cornerstone programs and also our cure violence groups in the neighborhood.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks which parts of the expanded 2017 Beacon model are working well and which need redesign. Asherall says the agency is still evaluating effectiveness and plans to take a deeper dive on all components as part of future planning.
chapters.title: Stevens presses on what aspects of 2017 Beacon model are working or need redesign

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The 2017 Beacon RFP significantly expanded expectation for providers by requiring them to function as youth development programs, family support centers, community hubs, referral networks, outreach organizations.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Nearly a decade later, what aspects of those models has DYCD concluded are working well, and what aspects would the agencies redesign if they were issuing a new RFP today?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Again, that's one of the things that we're planning to look at more closely to determine kind of like the effectiveness and figuring out like what makes most sense for the program moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So there's no place, none of these are, it's like, we should be moving on from, or you're still told to say you're still evaluating it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm just making sure because I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It can't be the answer to every question, right?

chapters.summary: Stevens asks for total Beacon enrollment in 2025 broken down by youth and adults, and whether any sites struggled with enrollment. Asherall provides FY25 figures: approximately 18,000 elementary, 17,000 middle school, 11,000 high school youth, and 17,000 adults, plus 126,000 community event attendees.
chapters.title: Stevens questions Beacon enrollment numbers and sites struggling to meet targets

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What were the total enrollment numbers for Beacon sites in 2025?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Can you break this down by youth and adults?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Were there any sites that struggled to meet enrollment targets?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: If so, what factors contributed to those challenges?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And this is the year 2025.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So I'll pass it to you to break it down with elementary math.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So in Beacons in fiscal year 25, we served 62,000 youth and 17,000 adults.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: How that breaks down.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so in fiscal year, do you want to broken up at each grade level, elementary, middle, and high?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, you can do that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And then when we follow up, you can send down a greater breakdown.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So for fiscal year 25, for beacons, we served over 17,000.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I'm rounding up, but I'm going to round up.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Close to 18,000 young people.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Middle school, 17,000.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: High school, 11,000.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And...

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Our adult population, over 17,000.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: They had community events is always a large number, and it's over 126,000 community members have attended activities or services offered.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: That was it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: No, okay.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Did you want something for the, I was wondering if you wanted for the beacons, right?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Specific to the beacons.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Those were just specifically beacons.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm getting into, I'm going to get into cornerstones now.

chapters.summary: Before transitioning to Cornerstone questions, Stevens explains NYCHA was not invited to the hearing to keep focus on programs, but acknowledges facility maintenance and repair issues are a major concern. She plans a future joint hearing with NYCHA.
chapters.title: Stevens previews Cornerstone questions and addresses NYCHA facility issues

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I just want to say before, while we're transitioning into cornerstones, one of the things we were...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Going to invite NYCHA to have a conversation about it, but then I felt like if we invited NYCHA, then this whole hearing would be about the repairs because that is such a real thing.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And that is probably the biggest issue.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And the thing that when I was in the community, it just drove me the craziest, right?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Like I had one center that had a roof that leaked, had another center every time it rained, it rained in this community center.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And all those issues are prevalent in a lot of community centers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And then we've been hearing even issues around like,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: you know, developments that turn rad pack, how, what's going on there.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And there's a lot.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So we did not bring them in because we didn't want the whole hearing to be about that, but we are looking to plan on having a deeper conversation with the NITRA chair and ourselves to see if we can have a more comprehensive hearing, because I do think we're at the point of like,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: there is not a lot of clarity about moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I say all that to say there are obviously going to be some questions about it, and I know what you're going to say is going to be like, well, that's a question you have to ask the nature.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I'm just preempting your response to the questions that we're going to ask.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about DYCD's process for resolving Cornerstone maintenance and safety issues with NYCHA. Asherall describes standing meetings, a centralized ticket system, dedicated triage vendors, and weekly coordination with NYCHA on structural issues.
chapters.title: Stevens questions DYCD coordination with NYCHA on Cornerstone facility maintenance

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I guess the first question I'm going to ask, cornerstone providers have repeatedly raised concerns regarding facilities.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: maintenance, security issues, access to community center space within NYCHA development.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Can DYCD describe the coordination with NYCHA to address those issues?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: When providers report maintenance or safety concerns, what is the process for resolving them and how does DYCD ensure that the communication between providers and NYCHA is timely and effective?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I will say this since I have left.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Which was four years ago.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Wait, I've been here five years.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But since I've not been in NITRA facilities, I've heard that it's a little bit better.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I did hear that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I heard it's a little bit better.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Not a lot, but a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I'll take it.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I'll take that little bit.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I will say this.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We have a really good, over the years when we started back in 2010, you know, there was obviously, you know, growing pains, right?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: But I think since then, 15 years, we just celebrated 15 years.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: 15 years later, we do have a really good working relationship with NYCHA.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We have standing meetings, monthly meetings, weekly meetings to speak specifically about facilities-related items.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We have a team on our end, our Youth Services Operations team, and within that team, they have folks that are dedicated for addressing any facility-related items.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We also have been very proactive in developing a centralized system that allows our CBOs to enter.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: tickets.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Into the system, and then we work towards resolving them.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And you guys are also tracking the tickets now?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yeah, we have.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: That's what I heard, that you guys are tracking the tickets.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Y'all were not doing that before.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: No, right.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So we are tracking that.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And we also have, we've been able to procure, like, I think we have, like, three vendors that are dedicated for triaging anything that comes through for us.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so they dispatch them out, and then the work can get addressed.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: There are, whenever there's a structural related, to your point, like I have leaks, I had a room with leaks and I had a building upstairs and it could have been somebody washing, I don't know, putting on their dishwasher, right?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so in those instances, we work really closely with NYCHA.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so those weekly meetings are really critical in addressing those facility-related issues that...

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Our vendors, nor DYCD, can address.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we work in tandem with NYCHA on those things.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, one of my challenges had always been, even when I was in the community center, like, there's residents who are also waiting to get their apartment fixed who live there.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And then it's like, I'm mailing about the community center.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so it's also a challenge of, like, how do they prioritize and what does that look like?

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about the Cornerstone RFP timeline and whether it would be combined with Beacons. Asherall says DYCD is exploring a combined community center RFP inclusive of both programs, but is still determining the best structure.
chapters.title: Stevens questions timeline for new Cornerstone RFP and potential combined procurement

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So my next question is, the YCD has not issued new cornerstone RFP since 2015.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What is the agency's timeline for releasing a new concept paper RFP for cornerstone?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And would that be something you guys would do in conjunction with weekends, or would that be separate, or what's the thinking?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Love that question.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Our goal is to release, as I mentioned earlier, a concept paper, and our commissioner really wants to be intentional about what is that right timeline to release those RFPs.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We are, as I mentioned earlier, examining the cost, the funding needs, stakeholder feedback, and then just the overall structure needed to support strong programming moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We want to allow that time to also be intentional about engaging the council and engaging all of our other partners as we develop that.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: But answer your question.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Right now, we are exploring the idea of a community center RFP so that it would be inclusive of Beacon and Cornerstones.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: But we also want to, as we're digging into that, we want to see whether or not that makes sense to release two solicitations within one RFP.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so those are the things that we're still kind of digging in a little bit more on as far as what that RFP could potentially be.

chapters.summary: Stevens raises 2022 testimony that Cornerstone providers lose money on contracts, with funding barely covering staffing. Asherall says DYCD is in conversations with OMB and City Hall about stabilization funding while awaiting a new RFP.
chapters.title: Stevens questions Cornerstone funding formula and provider losses

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: According to 2022 testimony before the committee, cornerstone providers reported losing money on their contracts.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They reported that funding often only covers a little more than their staffing and insufficient supports for OTPS expenses.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: for activities, trips, and partnerships, what considerations has DYCD given to redesign the cornerstone funding formula?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So we're in that process right now of looking at how do we stabilize the needs of these programs, both interchanging beacons and cornerstones, because they're just...

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: They were issued a long time ago.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: But we are looking at what is the funding need and what is the realistic cost to determine where there is an opportunity to do any stabilizations.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we are in conversations with OMB and City Hall to determine what that is while we wait for a potential concept paper and ultimately an RFP.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Stevens expresses frustration with the Cornerstone staffing model of one full-time director and mostly part-time staff, given the long operating hours. Asherall acknowledges directors function as 'mini EDs' and says staffing needs will be part of the upcoming assessment.
chapters.title: Stevens criticizes Cornerstone staffing model with single full-time director

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I mean, ultimately, with Cornstone specifically, I think I'm most frustrated at the staffing model and how there's typically one full-time director and everyone else is kind of part-time.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And anybody who knows that running a program, you need multiple people there for different times.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And then, like, when you're thinking about, like, the expectation around.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How often they're open, because I think sometimes we forget cornerstones are open all the time.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Like, all the time, because if you're a good cornerstone, you're being responsive to the community, and only having one full-time person becomes very difficult.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And then because the budget is already tight, if anyone comes in,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They then have to use ours.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And it just never works out.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And it just does not make sense.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So as we are thinking about the concept paper and thinking about how do we move forward, that is one of the things I definitely will be checking out and looking for because I just never understood staff model.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It was stupid.

chapters.summary: Stevens cites NYCHA's 600,000 open work orders and 444-day average repair time, asking if DYCD has assessed Cornerstone facility conditions. Asherall says DYCD's new centralized tracking system will help identify trends and timeliness of repairs.
chapters.title: Stevens questions DYCD assessment of Cornerstone physical conditions amid NYCHA repair backlog

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Cornerstone programs are intended to serve as community hubs within NYCHA development, given that NYCHA reports...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Reported more than 600,000 open work orders and average repair completion is of, dang, it's 444 days?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What assessments has DYCD conducted for the physical conditions of cornerstone facilities?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Do you guys, like, do evaluations of, like, all the cornerstones and have, like, stock of all the repairs?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So because we just have the centralized, we just, you know, it's been a year or so old.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We just developed that centralized system.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We are planning to do that.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so to look at that, to see what are the trends, the timeliness for addressing those facility-related issues.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we are looking at that currently just to see what are the trends and what are the things that we are seeing is coming up for programs on site.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about previously discussed plans to let Cornerstone providers handle basic maintenance rather than waiting for NYCHA. Asherall describes the ticket system, provider communication channels, monthly director meetings, and relocation strategies for severe issues.
chapters.title: Stevens questions status of division of maintenance responsibilities between DYCD and NYCHA

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I just have a couple more questions, and I'll pass it off to my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Council members, if you have questions, let me know so we can put you on the list.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You leaving?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Councilmember Joseph has joined us.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: She not going nowhere, so.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: NYCHA, DYCD, and OMB have previously discussed establishing a formal division of responsibilities that would allow cornerstone providers to address basic maintenance and repair needs rather than relying on NYCHA's...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Untangible wait times.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Providers have asked that DYCD ratify this division of responsibility and modify their budgets to ensure that they are not forced to secure resources from programming to cover basic maintenance.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What is the current status of this discussion?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I hate to say, to repeat that question.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: If you need me to repeat that, I can.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yes, can you please?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: NYCHA, DYCD, and OMB have previously discussed in establishing a formal division of responsibility that would allow cornerstone providers to address basic maintenance and repair needs rather than relying on NYCHA's long wait times.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so I guess it's what you were talking about, the database that you guys are using and trying to assess it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Where are you guys at with that of like trying to have folks being able to...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Get their stuff fixed without going through the night show?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yeah, we have, so our team, we have program managers, we have deputy directors, and so our providers are like, have us on speed dial, and so they do reach out to us regularly if there's something that

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: needs to be addressed or it's impacting program, and we try to be intentional and strategic about if necessary, like, and

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: if I miss anything, please chime in, but we try to be intentional and strategic if there is

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: a facility repair or issue that is affecting programming, we quickly activate and work with our other partners, our New York City public schools, to secure

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: a neighboring school across in the area that the families can relocate to minimize disruption of services, depending on the severity.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: But our CBOs regularly communicate.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We have monthly director meetings, and so we use those spaces for them to have a space to share any feedback.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And we've also...

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We've also implemented, like, I don't want to say customer service satisfaction, sorry, but surveys.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: To kind of like assess kind of like what we're hearing from programs.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Ms.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Harris, is there anything?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, okay.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It's on.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Oh, no.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay, well, you should turn it back off then.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because then you'll have a hot mic.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer raises the issue of long wait lists at the Goddard Beacon on the Upper West Side, where 20 families she knows cannot get in. Asherall acknowledges high demand and says DYCD is doing a geographical assessment to identify expansion needs.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer questions DYCD on Beacon wait lists at Goddard site

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: All right, I'm going to pause and pass it over to my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Is that Councilman Bufelder?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Councilman Bufelder, you have questions?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You pass?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
