utterances.text: Okay, well, he passed.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Well, there you go.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Councilman Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much for all your good questions.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: My question is on beacons.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I was Richard Murphy's best friend.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so I am very supportive, was there when they started.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So in my situation on the Upper West Side with Goddard, there's a waiting list, and it's been a whole year.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I must have 20 friends who have kids who go to work with them after school, literally, because they can't get into the beacon.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I complained to the chair.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That doesn't do any good.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I don't understand why.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: What's the purpose?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Murphy would fix this.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You've got to fix it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You can't have these kids, you know, going with their parents after school.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I literally have 20 friends who would like to go to that beacon.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So we have to wait for, like, years for an RFP?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How does this work?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I mean, it's just beyond frustrating.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They don't have the money for the Y.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They don't, you know, they want to go to the beacon.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: People love the beacon, but they can't get in.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I will say that Goddard is definitely has a long way.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: You know, it's very, very high.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: There's a high demand.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know, and I know all the people on the wait list.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yes, and the team at Goddard reaches out to us, and we try to...

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Problem solve and kind of be creative and kind of like how do we reach, how do we expand our reach for these families?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so that is definitely, there are some pockets of all of our centers where there's a huge and high demand.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And as we are looking and doing a geographical assessment to determine whether or not, where there is need to see where we can expand potentially.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so that's part of our deeper dive in doing an assessment of communities.

chapters.summary: Brewer presses on whether the holdup is funding or space and demands interim measures for wait-listed children. Asherall says DYCD wants to ensure equitable funding distribution and is assessing needs, while Stevens asks why additional slots haven't been added to high-demand sites.
chapters.title: Brewer presses on interim solutions for overcrowded Beacon sites

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm not very patient, so I have a question.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is this more money?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is it more space?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: What is the holdup?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I understand if you have to do an RFP and you've been talking about it, I got that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But in the interim, is it more money?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because there must be others that have high demand too.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I don't want to necessarily.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: say that here's the dollar amount that we would need because we have to do that.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I think we want to make sure that we look at what is the funding need?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: What do we need in order to properly fund these programs and potentially expand?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so that's the deeper dive that we need to do now so that when we do determine whether or not additional dollars are needed,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: or there's an actual need in this community, it's intentional.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We have data.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do you have some sense of how many have this kind of wait list?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is it, you know, like half or a third or something like that?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We would have to get back to you on that one.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But I don't, this could be years, what you're saying.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I mean, I'm just saying, years for this RFP.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: No, our commissioner is...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know she's on it, but meanwhile, literally 20 friends, I'm not kidding, when I say 20, are taking their kids after school to work with them because there's no other place for them to go.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I just have a question, too, because even to Councilmember Rose's question,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Often I know that there's times where even when there's contracts out and there is an RFP, if they see that it's a long wait list, DYCD has come in to add slots to programs and places.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so my question, why, especially if you're even saying, like, you know you're getting calls and you know the need, why haven't there been a consideration to add additional slots?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Is it a capacity thing?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Like, I'm trying to get an understanding because I don't feel like the question that Councilmember Brew's asking is being answered.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And I apologize if it hasn't been answered.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We want to make sure that it's equitable across the board.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we do need to do an assessment to determine where are these pockets and determine where we do need to add more dollars.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We want to make sure that it's a stabilized funding stream and it's equitable across the board.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we want to be intentional.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so that is the primary reason why we wouldn't necessarily say, okay, so this location is going to get additional dollars because then we'll be.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, no, I know that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But like, so in the last 20 years and when we're doing the evaluation, has there been evaluations to kind of see where there might be some programs that have less seats or who aren't meeting their enrollment and things like that?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because there has been shift in contracts.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Like, because I know you're saying like, oh, we're doing this evaluation.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I have ran these programs.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: There has been times where it was like, okay, we're shifting seats.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so, especially with these RFPs being so long, why hasn't that been done?

chapters.summary: Brewer insists she cannot wait years for an RFP and demands an interim plan for overcrowded sites. Stevens requests a citywide list of sites with wait lists for both Beacon and Cornerstone programs to inform funding decisions.
chapters.title: Brewer and Stevens continue pressing on Beacon wait list data and interim action

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And again, like, you know, Councilman Brewer actually has spoken to me about this for the last four years.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Endlessly.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so I'm just trying to get an understanding of like how and how are we evaluating even when we are still in the middle of a contract.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yeah, I think that these are really good questions, and I will definitely bring it back, and we can have internal conversations and obviously engage the council.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm going to bring Murphy down from heaven.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Literally.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: My question, though, is this is what I'm concerned about.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: These 20 kids, maybe they'll be okay, you know, but they'll be a lot better if they were at Beacon.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So then I wonder about all the other kids that are on wait lists.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They would be better off if they were at a Beacon.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I'm just thinking, you know, youth development.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's not great.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So while we're doing the RFP and figuring out what's next, they're sitting not in a great situation.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm worried about them.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I would love to see some interim, if that's what it takes, of the sites that are overcrowded or have a wait list.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know it doesn't make sense to me.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We're all focused on youth development, right?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This is, I mean, I know their chair is, I am.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And yeah, you and I, I am, you are.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm definitely focused.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And there are others.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But I'm just saying it doesn't make sense not to have some interim plan for this list.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So can you really look at it?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because it has been going on for a couple of years.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This is not a new thing.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's not like it's a new wait list.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: No, we'll definitely take that under advisement, especially as we are.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Doing this assessment to determine where there are the pockets.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I'm not good on assessments.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just want to make it happen.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So if it's more funding, I don't even think it's a space issue.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I think it is a funding issue.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I even go over there and say, excuse me, is that wait list moving?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I go over there and say, hello, Ray, can you move the wait list?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: These parents are, like, upset.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So, anyway, I don't want, I just want it.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about benchmarks for the right mixture of academics and fun in Beacon programs. Asherall explains the 2017 RFP was designed for flexibility, with DYCD now analyzing common activities and high-demand areas like workforce to inform future RFP language.
chapters.title: Brewer questions DYCD on benchmarks for academic and enrichment programming

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How are you also, my other question quickly is, how are you dealing with, like, benchmarks for the future?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Are there different benchmarks about what is the right mixture of academics and fun and so on?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How are you measuring those sorts of things?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And is it changing for the future?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yeah, well, the unique thing, as you know, about Beacon Community Centers is that we've designed the previous RFP in 2017 so that the CBOs could be flexible in their services.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Because every community is different and every need is different.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we are intentional about that.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we want to look at all the different...

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Like, what are the common activities that are being offered?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Where do we see a high demand across the board?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So that we can, when we're starting to think and engage our stakeholders and our partners, we can have data that says we're seeing a high demand of workforce.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we can then think about strategically infusing language in the RFP that speaks specifically to workforce.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Because there's data that shows across the system that workforce is one area of need.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we are taking that time now to look at all of the services.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: But every activity is different, right?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And every provider provides different types of services.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: But we are looking at those pieces currently.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Chair.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I really, will you get back to the chair about interim possibilities?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: for those that are overcrowded.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm sure there are others.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't think we're the only one.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I'd love to see.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I really am.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We can't wait another year.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm just saying.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They cannot be in people's offices for another year.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I would really like to see some interim measure.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I was going to say, if we can work with you guys, maybe we can get a list of all the sites across the city that have a wait list and try it so we can start to look at that because also that also some funding questions too.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And just for clarity, Beacon and Cornerstone?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yes, both.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Councilmember Joseph?

chapters.summary: Council Member Rita Joseph asks how DYCD measures whether programs achieve goals in academic outcomes, workforce readiness, and civic engagement. Asherall describes site visits, comprehensive monitoring tools, rubrics, and attendance-based reports.
chapters.title: Council Member Joseph questions DYCD on measuring program outcomes beyond participation

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Beyond participation countdown, how do DYCD measure whether Beacon and Cornerstone program are achieving their goals, improving academic outcomes, workforce readiness, civic engagement, and community well-being?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: You're welcome.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We, let's give me a moment.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: What metric do you have in place?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Just give me a moment.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I just want to make sure that I have the right information for you.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So currently what we do is, so we have a team of folks on the ground that conduct site visits.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we have a comprehensive monitoring tool that allows us to determine program quality, outcomes, and outputs.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We also look at administrative practices, and I'm missing one more, I'm sure.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: But we go out there.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We have a rubrics.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We have indicators, specific indicators attached to that comprehensive evaluation tool to determine.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Outcomes and to determine progress.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We also have our system where the providers enter their attendance and we are able to generate reports on, you know, rate of participation and overall engagement in programming.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks specifically about academic improvement measurement, tutoring, and reading levels. Asherall says DYCD does not track academic improvement per se but providers may do their own assessments. Stevens asks why academic outcomes aren't measured given the academic programming offered.
chapters.title: Joseph and Stevens press on academic outcome measurement and tutoring

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Let's talk about academic outcomes.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: When a young person comes to you, how do you measure if they've reached their goals in academic?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Is there tutoring?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Is there support?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Is there homework help?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Are they doing better?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: When they came to you, they were reading on a J level.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Have you moved their reading level?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Those are the things that I would like to hear.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Thank you for that clarity.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We don't necessarily...

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: look at academic improvement per se, but each provider on the ground may have their own assessment to determine whether there's been some form of improvement.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: But we don't necessarily look at whether their math scores have improved or their academic reading.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: The providers can tell whether or not a young person is no longer, from a youth development perspective, is not a person who's shy, is now more outspoken, is able to do public speaking.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so that's kind of like where it is, but we don't necessarily measure academic improvement per se.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And, and.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And how come you don't?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because I think you provide an academic arm to this program, right?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Right, we provide homework help.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We provide STEM activities.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: But we don't necessarily measure a young person's improvement.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: But we do offer those pieces.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We do offer academic enrichment in our services.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: All right.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks how DYCD measures workforce readiness and civic engagement outcomes. Asherall says individual providers track employment and resume-building data, and civic engagement activities like polling sites and youth councils are implemented at the provider level.
chapters.title: Stevens questions workforce readiness and civic engagement measurement

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What about workforce readiness?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How do you measure that?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Workforce readiness, have you placed young people in work or people who come to the program?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How have you done that?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So our providers on the ground that offer, and every program is different, not all of them provide workforce, but the ones that do offer, they do have a system on site.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So every provider may have.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Data on, you know, a young person was employed now, they're now able to, you know, they have their resume building, so they're able to do some of that and they measure it on their own.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And your civic engagement, what does that look like?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How many folks you register to vote, how many folks you get to the poll, how does that work?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So, again, I hate to repeat myself, but, yes, each provider, we offer those opportunities of civic engagement, and we offer exposure for our providers to take advantage of these things, but the providers on their own are the ones that actually implement it, and they invite us in those places.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I don't know if I'm missing.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Board of Elections is another piece.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You have to put on your mic so I can hear you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: So at Cornerstones, there is also access provided by partnering with Board of Elections and making sure that our polling sites are available to residents, seniors, and also young people outside of their regular schools.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: So that's another way that we infuse it or through themes and just...

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: Coaching our providers to make civic engagement part of like youth council, advisory board, that kind of thing.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks how DYCD evaluates adult programming like GED prep, ESL, and parenting workshops. Asherall says providers report GED graduations but DYCD lacks comprehensive data. Stevens emphasizes that tracking GED completions could drive funding and policy decisions.
chapters.title: Stevens questions evaluation of adult programming including GED and ESL

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How does DYCD evaluate the effectiveness of your adult programming, including GED preparation, ESL classes, parenting workshops, and workforce development activities?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: The programs that offer GED services have communicated to us that their adults have graduated or received their GED.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I've even had the pleasure of visiting some of our programs.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: How many have you graduated already?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Oh, I would need to.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You could get back to us.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yeah, I would need to definitely do an assessment across all of our providers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, because then you've just, that's also part of the economic mobility, right?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You've just given somebody their GED.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They can now apply for college.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They can go to a trade school.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They can move on to the next career.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Those are wins you can celebrate as well, right?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But if you don't have no data, that doesn't drive your policy.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: That's okay.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It doesn't help drive your policy and even your funding because you may need more funding because you may need more GED seats.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You may need more ESL seats.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And parenting workshops.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Can you talk to me a little bit about that?

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about parenting workshops and literacy components for parents. Threcia Harris describes partnerships with NYCHA for community baby showers, library card drives, book distributions, and author events. Asherall adds co-locators provide family engagement programming.
chapters.title: Stevens and Harris discuss family engagement and literacy components

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Our providers, family, adult and family engagement is a core piece of the community centers.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so our programs provide, you know, workshops for families.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: They do kind of like mommy and me workshops sometimes.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: They've done...

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I don't want to call it a sip and paint, but like, you know.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I know you give out apple cider, right?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yeah, apple cider.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Apple cider, thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And painting.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So we've seen some of that.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We've also offered to some of our programs opportunities for them to receive additional resources, like a co-locator to come in and do a family engagement component.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And I'm going to pass it on over to my colleague, Ms.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Harris, who can share some more insight.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: Well, also we partner with, in the cornerstones at least, NITRA-REES family engagement programs to bring the community baby showers, to bring some of the morning services, although we open in the afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: You may see a few where needed parenting workshops in the mornings.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: And beacons on Saturdays also engage families in that way.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Is there any component of it where there's a literacy aspect to it, where parents are encouraged to read with their children?

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: I'm not going to say it's a requirement, but we've seen it.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: The reading programs, libraries will come in.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: There is that partnership with New York Public Library to get your library card or to do some of the bringing in characters and doing that kind of family engagement.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And also the book.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: Oh, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: With NYCHA, we have also done a BRE book.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: Rich environments and there's thousands of books given across the NITRA development.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: We also partner with

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: cornerstones and compass programs to make sure young people have that.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: There's an author event where the author is literally signing books and young people are coming in with their parents or without, and they leave with books.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: So literacy has been infused in different ways through different partners.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Is there a chair?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Knock myself out?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks what performance metrics are used and how often providers are reviewed. Harris says scored evaluations occur at least three times per year, with additional coaching and support as needed. Asherall adds strategic and corrective action plans are used for underperforming sites.
chapters.title: Stevens questions performance metrics and provider review frequency

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How do you, what performance metric are you using now to measure the providers?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How often are providers reviewed for contract compliance and program quality?

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: So at a minimum, three times a year, scored evaluation.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: Summer, we look at the fall, the spring, and then we do other assessments in between.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: If a provider needs additional coaching or support, that's where we step in, but we're measuring their...

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: Contractual requirements in terms of stats and numbers, but also in terms of just the quality of moving from one place to the other, the number of families they're engaging, that kind of thing.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: So they are assessed.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: I couldn't give you a stat right now, but...

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: Right, right.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And I want to add, just to add to that, in addition to those various touch points, there's also frequent kind of like on-site coaching.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We also, depending on what's also what's happening on the ground, right, there's some providers that, you know,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: could use some improvement.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we may implement a strategic action plan or a corrective action plan.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And then we are really intentional about looking at that plan and following it in a way to see progress.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we're really detailed in that way.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And then we also have our capacity building division who has numerous vendors that can do either on-site, one-on-one consultation, either

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: at the site level or at the agency level.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And that's in addition to like whatever.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: workshops are being made available for anyone to attend, any group leader.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: To support the nonprofit as a provider.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks what specific milestones providers and the Council should expect in the next 12 months regarding Beacon and Cornerstone procurement. Asherall says to expect intentionality around timeline, stakeholder engagement, and transparency, culminating in a concept paper and RFP.
chapters.title: Stevens questions DYCD on 12-month milestones for future procurement

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I have one last question for you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So what specific milestones should provider and council expect over the next 12 months regarding the future procurement of Beacon and Cornerstone community centers?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What should we expect?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So what you should expect is definitely more intentionality about really looking at, like, what is a realistic timeline to release an RFP?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We're still wrapping up our compass.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so really looking at an intentional timeline to release an RFP, we want to be really intentional about stakeholder engagement.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We know one of the lessons that we've learned in the current process is, like, the importance of family engagement and schools and RAs and stuff like that.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: It's a huge footprint, but we want to make sure that we allow that time and that grace to kind of like look at what are the trends, what are people saying, and how do we infuse.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And we may not make everybody happy, right?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: But at least we could be intentional about having data.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Making sure that we engage all of our partners, and then being able to be transparent with kind of like what we're planning to put out there.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And then that'll be the concept paper and ultimately an RFP.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Ms.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Harris, you wanted to say something, or you're good?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks how DYCD will engage NYCHA residents and school stakeholders in the future procurement process. Asherall describes past RA engagement as RFP readers, a current RA survey, and the need to identify better touch points for community input.
chapters.title: Stevens questions how DYCD will engage NYCHA residents and school stakeholders in procurement

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Well, I'm happy to hear that some of the lessons that you learned, because I was about to ask what were some of the lessons that you guys are learning from this current RP that we're still in the middle of.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It's not over yet.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: That we're still in the middle of.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I'm happy to hear that that parent engagement piece, how it was kind of lacking and thinking about, like, moving forward, how do we make sure that we have more of a holistic view around what does that process look like?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so that's my next question that I have.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How will the UICD engage?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: NITRA residents, and this is mostly for the Cornerstones school, NITRA resident school stakeholders and other large communities in the evaluation process and the procurement process moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yeah, we need to, it's something that I'm even like,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: trying to figure out because what does that what would that look like so that you know there's so many people that may want to have a say I know that what I can say is this that last time we issued an RFP for our cornerstone

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: we did have intentional RA engagement and so what did that look like that looked like they were readers we asked them to be part of the selection process and so you know

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We have, I think we had 100 at the time, or maybe less than that.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I don't think we had 100 yet.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so out of the 75 potential,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: RAs that we could have had.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We only had a handful of RAs that, you know, were willing to kind of commit to being an evaluator for an RFP.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So we still need to figure out what that touch point is.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Right now, we've also issued a survey.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: To the RAs to get feedback from them.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Because to your point, we've been there for a while.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we did issue a survey to kind of get their input of like, what are they seeing?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: What do they want to see more of or less of?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so that information, I'm hoping that it'll help inform

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: you know, the direction.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I think one, we have to be at a place where we're doing better at getting feedback from community members and key stakeholders like parents and principals and things like that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So that can be used part of things moving forward so we could have it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But also even when we're doing a new procurement, like we have to make sure that we're getting

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: people's feedback and I think that that's important and I think that that was one of the missteps and hiccups that we had with this RP

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: for the after school but I think that was totally different because y'all didn't even have that much time so don't get me started there but I do want to

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: us to be thinking about moving forward, how do we make sure that different voices are heard in these conversations.

chapters.summary: Stevens raises concerns about last-minute requests for providers to host NBA Finals watch parties, asking about notice protocols and additional compensation. Asherall says providers were given a choice and that DOE helped with security and custodial staff.
chapters.title: Stevens questions DYCD on last-minute NBA Finals watch party requests to providers

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Providers reported that they received a last-minute request to host watch parties for Game 3, which I'm sure Dave is excited about, for the NBA Final Series.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Again,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And this becomes an issue.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Does the DYCD have a formal protocol or best practices around how much prior notice they give providers before making requests for these special events?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And are there additional compensation for these groups and things like that to have to get staffed?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because this goes back to my staffing pattern.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: When you only have one full-time person, then that person typically is the only one opening up these places.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So just trying to get a better understanding of those type of things.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So, you know, the Knicks, they won, and we worked really closely with our partners at the New York City Public Schools,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: you know, to kind of collaborate on leveraging the fact that our corners, our beacons are already open extended hours during the school year.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we kind of, they were already in the building, they were already providing services.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we kind of leaned in on that ability to do that.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So they already had their staff.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And the thing that was unique and beneficial for us was that it was just one location.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so the DOE helped us with security, making sure security was there.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: They helped us secure custodial staff to make sure that the space and the school building was readily available for instruction the next day.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so it was a collaborative approach to make sure that our communities.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: had access as a community, like true beacons and cornerstones do, they created that space.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so the win for me is that the school administration and New York City Public Schools saw this as like, oh, wow, look at our beacons.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: They're core.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Part of our community and here's what they did for our community.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So that was, I think the timeline, we worked aggressively in identifying schools and we gave the providers a choice.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We basically said, hey, this is an opportunity, are you interested?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so the ones that- I hate when we say that when a funder comes and asks a provider to do something.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And some do say no, but that's not often because, and the person saying no typically isn't, the person saying yes typically isn't the person opening the program either.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So, you know, I think it's kind of hard.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I just, because I remember those situations.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The ED is going to say, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The ED ain't opening the building.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So it just becomes hard.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And again, this goes back to my issue around the staffing, right?

chapters.summary: Stevens raises provider concerns about needing three leadership positions to prevent burnout, with most sites unable to afford it. Asherall confirms staffing structure and funding needs will be part of the upcoming assessment, acknowledging directors function as 'mini EDs.'
chapters.title: Stevens questions DYCD on staffing structure and burnout for program directors

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So my next question on providers and both cornerstones of the...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Beacons report that they need three, at least three leadership positions to staff to balance out the need to have management positions on site at all times to prevent burnout.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: However, most sites are not able to support three leadership positions due to budget constraints and meaning that program directors are often stretched thin, working excessive hours.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Have DYCD evaluated the staffing structures in regards to the responsibilities and hours of these programs?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Will DYCD consider increasing program budgets to address this issue and receive adequate staff, program staff?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: That is going to be part of the assessment that we're hoping to gather.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And that will help determine the actual funding need and staffing needs to sustain.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: To what I said earlier about community centers don't just do after school.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Oh, I know that.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I know what I'm saying.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Do all the things.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Exactly.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: People think they just do that.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I always say that our beacons and our cornerstone directors are mini EDs.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I often say that.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: They're mini EDs.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We are literally running around.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Not only are they EDs, they're also the janitor, the cook.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Right, right.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: All the things, the repairman, all the things, the plumber.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so when we start doing our assessment and our focus group conversations and our surveys, those are the kind of things that we are going to be looking for and asking for.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about underutilization of extended late-night hours and staff burnout from working 8am to 11pm. Asherall says DYCD is analyzing attendance data by time slot to determine realistic timeframes and strategies to avoid burnout.
chapters.title: Stevens questions evaluation of late-night program hours and staff burnout

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: This is another one that happened when I was in community centers that was nice, but this is what burnt me out.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: In the last few years, the evening hours for both Cornerstones and Beacons have been extended to late to 11 p.m.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: To provide safe space for community members, specifically in the summer.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Many programs report that those late hours underutilized.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Underutilization by the community system and expectation of sites that have gyms.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: facilities have DYCD evaluated the impact of utilization of the program sites in late night hours.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And again, this was something that I remember when I was running program, we got, we loved, and it was important.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But again, because of the destruction model, like, and because most directors, even if you have other people in the building, you want to be there because it's your building.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: We get unhealthy attachments.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so, you know, you end up being there from 8 in the morning when you have summer camp to 11 o'clock at night and then starting all over again.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And then when we got the extended weekends, we were there on the weekends.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so I remember basically living in a community center and three community centers for three summers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So just trying to, like, how do you guys evaluate, how you evaluate that and making sure that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: These programs are being utilized and that the staff isn't being totally burnt out.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yeah, we try to work with our providers in offering strategies to avoid burnout.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we do that and we offer information sessions on those pieces.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: What I will say is that

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: you know, those, we are, we've started looking at attendance data, and we started, like, looking at it from, like, different time slots to determine the kind of, like, what is a realistic, and I think to,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: brewer like thinking about where are those need

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: and where we're seeing a lot of traffic after a certain time frame and where we're seeing less traffic to determine what is that sweet spot.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So we started looking at the data, and I think that we're going to be able to take a deeper dive once we start really looking at the...

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Holistic design, you know, because the summer is, those summers are longer than the school year, right?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I mean, you know, so we're looking at the summer component and we're looking at the school year component and trying to figure out what are, what does the, what is the attendance telling us to determine what is that realistic timeframe?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Those summer hours are long, but I won't lie.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Those are the best summers I had.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I don't think I could do it again in this old age, but good times.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks how DYCD collaborates with DOE to support students with disabilities in Beacon and Cornerstone programs. Asherall says programs make reasonable accommodations and that Cornerstone staff have access to DOE's inclusive practice workshops from Summer Rising.
chapters.title: Stevens questions DYCD on serving students with disabilities

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How does DYCD collaborate with DOE to support programs with students with disabilities?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I will say this.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Although I am not a fan of Summer Rising, everybody knows that I've never made a secret of it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But I think one of the things that we really did benefit from summarizing is just seeing how students with disabilities should be serviced and being able to provide services.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And it wasn't so much unnecessarily, I feel like, in this RFP for after school, which I'm a little disappointed by, but like thinking about cornucopias and beacons moving forward, how do we kind of make sure that we are...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Including students with disabilities in this conversation and making sure that they are prioritized when we're thinking about these RFPs and rollout.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because again,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It gets really hard for parents who with students with disabilities, they typically don't have programs to put their kids in.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Well, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: That is definitely something that is at the core of our, you know, of the commissioner and kind of like looking across the spectrum.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: The goal is to make every reasonable accommodation that ensures that every participant

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: has an opportunity to engage.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so all of our programs are dedicated to working with young people of various disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we recognize the importance of addressing the unique needs of each individual to foster their growth and participation.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We, you know, to your point about summer rising, I think, you know, one of the wins for me has been just the menu of offerings and workshops and info sessions that the DOE has made available.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so although our cornerstone programs are not part of summer rising, right, we try to create spaces for

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: our cornerstone directors and staff to receive that information and learn about best practices and strategies of creating an inclusive environment during after school and evening hours.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So that's something that we're definitely committed to in ensuring that we infuse in our work.

chapters.summary: Stevens questions why Beacons are required to do workforce development but cannot use stipends as an allowable cost. Asherall does not have an immediate answer and commits to following up, while Stevens argues stipends and jobs are critical for youth engagement.
chapters.title: Stevens questions why stipends are not allowable costs for Beacon workforce development

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Another question I got from providers were that the beacons are required to do workforce development, but we don't allow stipends as a...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: allowable costs, trying to get an understanding why would that be?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Why is that not something that we're encouraging and trying to do more where there is workforce development happening in these programs and young people are interning or, you know, getting specific skills?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Why can't they allow stipends?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I unfortunately don't have that response.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I definitely will get back to you on that piece for the stipend.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I bet you will.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Andrew, write that down.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I want to know why.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I don't want to mess it up, so that's the reason I'm holding it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: No, I think that that's important.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I think, you know, when we're looking at the unemployment rates for young people, specifically between the ages of 16 and 24, they're at the highest since COVID and have not come down.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so especially for programs, we should be looking at encouraging young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And sometimes those young people who don't come to programs because they are trying to make money.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so we should be encouraging it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so if and I don't know, I don't know how to have money after these begin budgets because they're so tiny.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So they got the money.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But if they can, they should be able to eke out and be able to give the young people some stipends.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And I know we also work closely with Valerie and her team to try to, you know, create, I mean, with DYC, right?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so we definitely try to.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Work closely with our workforce team to see where there are opportunities for some of our beacon participants to attend like workforce center or any other stipend related.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, but I feel like especially

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: When we got the kids, we got to keep them because sometimes when you change them from program to program, you lose them.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I'm going to tap in Ms.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Harris.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Go ahead, Ms.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Harris.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I want to give you my practice.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: In some of our programs as well, not directly through DYCDR providers, but like the ONS, CMS providers sometimes have.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: The ability to infuse stipends for young people, 16 to 24, and job.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I know, but it's not enough.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Not at all.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: We always need more.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: No, it's not even that we need more.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It's just not enough.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Those stipends sometimes are $100.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And don't get me started on that.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: But those are the things that I think that that's the feedback that we want to hear.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And I think that when we go back and we look at our policy and be able to kind of like get back to you on that, I think that'll help.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, again, like I've been yelling at the rooftop around how we need more employment opportunities for young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And again, I want to also be clear, when I'm talking to young people, they don't want stipends anymore.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They said they want jobs.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They said they want to be able to have hours.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They actually are annoyed with the stipends because it's like less or whatever, but they want real jobs.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so we should be responsive to that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But I think even if there's programs where they're doing workforce development and they're like, hey, I want to give them a $100 stipend for coming to these five workshops, that should be allowable.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And allowing them to have that flexibility with their budget.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks why Beacon participants must attend three days before being considered enrolled, when no similar requirement exists for Cornerstone or Compass. Asherall and Harris confirm the requirement exists but cannot explain its origin, noting it will be reviewed.
chapters.title: Stevens questions three-day attendance requirement for Beacon enrollment

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The next question I have, which I did not know, and this is a question for providers, why is there a requirement for participants to participate three days before being considered to enroll when there is no similar requirements for Cornerstone or Compass?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: That's happening in the begins.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yes, that is true.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: It is in the current program design that was, you know, 2017.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: And so that is one of the things that we're looking at.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I don't know how we got there.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It's so random.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: I can't remember how we got to that space.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: We can definitely dig.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: No, you had your light on.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Don't turn it off.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: I just wanted to ask a clarifying.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: You said three days of participation?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So remember, three days of participation.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They have to attend three days before they're considered enrolled.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: It's beacon.

utterances.speaker: Threcia Harris
utterances.text: Just, okay.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: So, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Asherall
utterances.text: Yeah, we can't speak to the logic how we got to that space, but I will say that that's one of the areas that we're looking at.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: We'll add that to the list of things we want to change.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I think those are all the questions that I...

chapters.summary: Council Member Brewer delivers brief closing remarks emphasizing the need for robust, accessible youth development, particularly at NYCHA developments, and expresses urgency about addressing the needs of young people not currently in programs.
chapters.title: Brewer gives closing remarks on urgency of youth development

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer, Councilmember Felder, no question?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay, well.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Just thank you very much, but again, I know we're talking about the overcrowded, but just in general, data and support for youth development that is targeting, as the chair knows, those young people that are not

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: in programs, you know, when I don't think we're making them accessible enough.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And it's, I know you're trying and you've got many other agencies that you're working with, particularly at NYCHA developments, we need to have just robust youth development.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I know you're working on it, but I don't feel this imminent, you know,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: emergency need, that's what I'm looking at.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's what I'd like to see.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Stevens thanks the DYCD panel, jokes about being efficient, and transitions to public testimony, outlining the rules for public speakers including two-minute time limits and written testimony submission procedures.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens dismisses administration panel and opens public testimony

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I don't have any more questions for you guys.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I think that was pretty painless.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So at this time, I don't have any more questions for this panel.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Look, you did great.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I was nice, right?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I told y'all I'd be fast.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: See, Andrew texted me outside.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I said, oh, mama, be quick.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: He said, wrap it up.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So thank you, guys.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm now open the floor to public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Before I begin, I remind members of the public that this is a formal government proceeding and that decorum.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: shall be observed at all times.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: As such, members of the public shall remain silent at all times.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The witness table is reserved for people who wish to testify.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: No radio recordings or photography is allowed from the witness table.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Further, members of the public may not present audio or video recordings as testimony, but they may submit transcripts of such recordings to the Sergeant of Arms for inclusion in the hearing records.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: If you're wishing to speak today,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Please fill out an appearance card with the Sergeant of Arms and wait for your name to be called.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Once your name is recognized, you will have two minutes to speak about the oversight topic or the resolutions listed on today's agenda.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: If you have written testimony or additional written testimony you wish to submit for the record, please provide a copy of the testimony to the Sergeant of Arms.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You may also email your written testimony to testimony at council.nyc.gov within 72 hours of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Please note that audio and video recordings will not be accepted.

chapters.summary: Assemblymember Landon Diaz testifies in support of Resolution 142, sponsored by Council Member Kevin Riley, calling for a state Center for Fatherhood Initiatives. He shares his personal experience as a father, discusses the need for resources on postpartum depression and co-parenting, and notes the bill passed the Senate but stalled in the Assembly.
chapters.title: Assemblymember Diaz testifies in support of Resolution 142 on Center for Fatherhood Initiatives

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: When you hear your name, please come to the witness panel for the first panel.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I believe the first panel is Assemblymember Diaz, who will be talking about a resolution we have today.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: He's from Harlem.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just want to let you know.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: No, no, no.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The Bronx claims him.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: He is from Harlem.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just wanted to know.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know his parents very well.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm from Harlem, too, but we the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: No, no, no.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Us Harlem people are in the Bronx, so please let her know.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: We are.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I did just come from Harlem, so.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Enough of you, Lantana.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Enough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: First off, good afternoon to the council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I want to say thank you for having me here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I am speaking on behalf of Kevin Riley's resolution 142, which is for the Favorhood Initiative, which is in support of a bill that we have in Albany, which is Assembly Bill 89811, which is the Favorhood Initiative bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Myself and Senator Jamal Bailey, he has it in the Senate, sir.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Being a father is one of the greatest gifts I've ever had.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: My two boys are actually with my father right now, visiting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: They just arrived in Atlanta to visit my older brother, their uncle, Lareek.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And my father and my older brother were two great examples that I've had in my life, what it means to be a father.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: But I am, yes, you do know my parents very well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: But I am a very wealthy man because I have two great parents and a father that came from very little but had a foundation of love for my grandmother, and he bestowed that upon us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: What I do know is the young men in New York don't have that same wealth that I have when it comes to that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: The fatherhood initiative is to create.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: create a resource center for the young men or the young fathers within the state of New York who need access.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And even with all the resources I had within my own family, I'll be the first person to say when my wife was pregnant, there wasn't a lot of resources for fathers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I didn't know about postpartum depression.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I didn't know about preeclampsia, where if not for, for example, Hall of Fame tennis player Serena Williams'husband intervening when she was giving birth, we might have lost the greatest tennis player of all time

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: because her pleas of pain weren't being heard and the father of our children had to step in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: So I want to make sure that the young fathers within the state of New York have this knowledge, that they understand that what does a woman go through in those situations and how that they could be a better partner and a better father.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Even if they're not together, co-parenting is still key when it comes to New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: We want to make sure that the young people and the children, the future of New York, have parents that have as much resources as possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: As you had in the previous testimony, it's not just about fatherhood, but also making sure that they have workforce development programs and making sure that these young fathers have apprenticeship programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: They can get into programs so they can provide.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: for their children so that they can have housing and education and beacon programs and after school programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I was, I'll be the first to say that the Senate passed the fatherhood initiative and I'm still kind of dismayed on why we couldn't get it done in the assembly and I will go, I'll go at it again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I've also had conversations with Commissioner Harris of OCFS, and she's in full support of it also, because there are young fathers who are currently in

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: juvenile centers that still need to be within their children's lives, and these resources could be used to help it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: To the chairwoman, I know her advocacy for young people is ever essence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: She works her tail off in our district in the Bronx where we overlap, but for all New York City families.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And I think the fatherhood initiative and this resolution is key.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I want to thank Kevin Riley for bringing this to the table.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: But more importantly, I really hope that we can create a broad coalition across the political spectrum, across the boroughs, religions or whatever to.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Embrace this because I think strengthening young fathers across the state and New York City is how we create not just that's how we make New York better and stronger.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And I want to thank the council for hearing my testimony in this.
