utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And I hope that we can count on you guys on creating a coalition to support our bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And I want to thank again for you guys for passing this resolution.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: So thank you kindly.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks Diaz about coordinating with DYCD's city-level fatherhood programs, including 17 new sites. Diaz describes his barbershop series with Senator Bailey and Council Member Riley, and they agree to connect with DYCD and other dad-focused programs across the city.
chapters.title: Stevens and Diaz discuss connecting city fatherhood initiatives with state efforts

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you, Senator, for being here.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I guess I have a couple questions.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: One, I spoke to the OCFS commissioner as well, and she's also talking about rolling this out statewide.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Before the office is up and running.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: She definitely believes in this.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But my question to you is, how are you working with the fatherhood initiative at the city level?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I know DYCD, we had a major rollout last year where I believe it's like 17 additional sites and really trying to do this.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Have you been working with them?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Is that something that you haven't been connected with?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: If not, I would love to connect you and have you heard about the work that they've been doing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: So please connect me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: What we have done is a barbershop series with Senator Bailey and Councilman Riley.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: We did one right near River Park Towers, which we overlap, and it was very successful.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: We had over 50 young men show up, and it was great dialogue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And talking about co-parenting and other legal rights that they have as fathers, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Why, if they're traveling to Nashville, why you need a notarized letter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: You know, if you have an issue with child support, what can you do?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Also been working with, as a lawyer with legal services, to make sure that young fathers have legal representation when they go to family court.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Because sometimes the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: The judicial interference of families is a conversation I know you've had with ACS and other oversight that you've been pushing for.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And so I think that conversation has been tremendously helpful, and it is at least getting some...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I saw legitimate and tangible results in that short period.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And by expanding it to your point, I think it will only do more for our city.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, they did an additional 17 across the city.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The two in the Bronx, I believe, is closest to you is Claremont Center.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And then also, I think it's Metropolitan College.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I make sure I connect you with them as well.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: In addition to I make sure that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I connect you with Bob.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I connect you with Metropolitan College.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I do apologize, but I would love to do a tour with you and maybe with Kevin.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Let's do it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Kevin had the gang, the dad gang.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I had the brunch of dads and there's other dad programs that have been blooming across the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And I would love to connect them and really create some momentum.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, because I think there are some resources there.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And even, like I said, the conversation I had with the OCFS commissioner, she was really excited about it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Also, like, how do we connect it?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because a lot of times, a lot of all these things are already happening.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And then it's like, how do we connect the dots to have the biggest impact for all of it?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because there is a lot of moments around, like, fatherhood initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so for me, it's like, well, why are we all not working together?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I will make sure that we have a conversation.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'll bring Bob and everyone from DYCD together so we can start talking about what does it look like holistically.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I know Councilman Riley is also trying to get an office of fatherhood open at the city as well.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I think that's one of his priorities this year as well.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I will be due diligent and make sure I connect you because I know where to find you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And I will support Kevin, sorry, Councilman Riley on that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And the one big thing I would like to just put on the record, one reason why I think this is more important, on the internet, there is this...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I'll be honest, this toxic masculinity sometimes that has been perpetuated on social media, and we're trying to counteract that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: In reality, fathers today are one of the most interactive fathers in generations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: My kids will tell you they love when I cook.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Well, Gail, I know you like my cooking also, but...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It's all right.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I love his cooking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: It's all right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Althea be hating on my cooking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: But we've changed.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because I think it's better.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: That's what I'm saying.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: That's not.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: This is on the record, ma'am.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: We have people.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: People are watching.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Behave.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: But more importantly, though, we are changing the narrative on what fatherhood looks like.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And it's different examples, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And I think that when they see that, you might fit into a different mode of what it is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: But at the end of the day, as long as you're providing love and empathy and caring for your children, I think that is what needs to be magnified.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And I think you're doing it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I think Gale's always been doing it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I know the senators talk about family values also.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Sorry, the councilman has talked about family values also.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I'm just used to calling him senator.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: But this is something I'm very passionate about, and thank you so much for that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, there was a father advocate that was here.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I think he left already.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: That comes to a lot of my hearings, too.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: If he comes back, I'll make sure I connect you because he's come to talk about his struggles as well and trying to bring fathers together as well.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: He was here, but he left out.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I'll definitely connect you to him as well.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So thank you so much for being here today.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Oh, Councilman Brewer has some questions.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about CUNY programs for fathers affected by federal funding cuts. Diaz commits to working with CUNY and discusses changing the narrative around Black and brown father involvement, citing data that contradicts stereotypes about absent fathers.
chapters.title: Brewer questions Diaz on CUNY fatherhood programs and changing fatherhood narratives

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Assemblymember.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: CUNY, City University of New York.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Are you working with them?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because they had a horrible situation with the feds cutting some of their funding for this issue, and I don't know where they're at.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I was wondering if you also work with some of the fathers there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I will.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: We had conversations trying to put back funding for CUNY.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Obviously, SUNY and CUNY took tremendous hits, and we did our best in this current state budget.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: You know, we did get our budget done, unlike the

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: council yet, but that's true.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: You guys fast years on time, unlike us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: But I will have those conversations because I think in our education, in our education facilities, I think putting this as a part of it is key.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And also making sure that single fathers or single mothers or single parents in general have also child care opportunities at those places so they can further their education and their jobs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: But I also think there's something, you know, I think there's something amazing if the same schools, even starting in high school and bringing back home ec to a small degree on how to change a diaper, how to swaddle, how to prepare a bottle.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: I think all those things are life lessons that we all need to learn, regardless if you plan to have a family or not, because you might be an uncle or aunt, a godmother, a godfather, or a godparent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And I think all those skills help us as a village to come together.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: So I will work with City College and the CUNY system, and thank you for bringing that to my attention.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And the Times did have an article the other day about how much more fathers are involved than they were when I was growing up.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yes, I think you're on.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Obviously, you just need to make sure that people have the support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Yes, and further to that knowledge, one thing that I want to also make sure that we make public knowledge is that not only that they have actually shown that black and brown fathers are actually one of the most proactive in changing the public perception of the absent black and brown father.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And we actually know through the periodical data that it's actually not true.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: And we need to make sure that we echo that from coast to coast, from city to the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: to the farms because we need to let people know that farmers are very active in 2026 and we need to continue that process.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Assemblymember.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilwoman Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I don't have any more questions for this panel, so thank you for joining us, Ms.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Sussman.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairwoman.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilman Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilman Felder.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'll be calling the next panel, Christy Mansell, David Garcia, Pedro Pez, Kate Connolly, Annie Nelson.

chapters.summary: Christy Mansell, Division Director for Center for Family Life in Sunset Park, testifies about operating two Beacon programs since 2003. She calls for funding rates that reflect true costs, stipends for youth interns, and community voice in new Beacon design.
chapters.title: Christy Mansell testifies on Beacon programs as community anchors in Sunset Park

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: We'll make sure that you're safe.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You guys can get started, whoever wants to go.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: Good afternoon and thank you Chair Stevens and members of the New York City Council for the opportunity to provide testimony today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: My name is Christy Mansell and I am a Division Director for School-Based Programs for the Center for Family Life in Sunset Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: I'm here to speak about the critical role that Beacons and Cornerstone programs play in our communities and about the need for the city to protect and adequately fund these models as true community building programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: I've been working in or overseeing beacon programs for the Center for Family Life since 2003.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: We operate two DUI City Beacon programs in Sunset Park, Brooklyn, and I believe they are vital resources to our neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: Our beacon programs are anchors in the community, serving people across age groups and building trust over many years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: Our beacon programs are places where many people find belonging and connection with their community, which combats isolation in these challenging times.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: Community members not only learn about and access resources from the Beacon staff, they also become resources for each other, strengthening the fabric of our neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: The Beacon model allows providers to create unique community spaces that are both reflective of and responsive to the community we serve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: They are welcoming spaces for new New Yorkers, long-time residents, young people, families, and older adults.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: They are not solely after-school programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: From ESL instruction and Know Your Rights workshops to soccer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: leagues, dance troops, and family cooking classes, there is something for every member of the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: And if there isn't, there is flexibility to create, host, or partner with neighborhood groups to bring new programming to life as new needs emerge.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: Our programs are being asked to meet real community needs while operating within funding structures that do not reflect the current cost of the work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: Rates need to reflect what it actually takes to run high-quality, safe, responsive programs today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: This means investing in the workforce that makes this work possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: The jobs are hard with late nights and weekend hours, and programs are often asked to extend hours, pivot quickly, and respond to urgent community needs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: Staff, the majority who are community members and former participants in the Beacon themselves, rise to those requests, so the city must recognize that what is being asked of these programs and invest resources to support them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: The city should also support the leadership pipeline within Beacons.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: Many participants volunteer their time as counselors in training or as youth coaches because the program has been meaningful in their lives and they want to contribute to their community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: That commitment is powerful, but they should not have to work for free.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: We should be able to provide stipends for interns and create paid career readiness pathways for young people who want to give back to their communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: Finally, community voice must be central in any new beacon design process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: Families, participants, and school partners should have a real voice in describing the value of their beacon and shaping its future.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christy Mansell
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: David Garcia shares his personal story of growing up as a Beacon participant and now serving as a co-director of a Beacon program. He describes Beacons as safe spaces that build community and shape future leaders, urging continued investment.
chapters.title: David Garcia testifies on his journey from Beacon participant to co-director

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to speak today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: My name is David Garcia.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: I'm here to talk about the importance and impact of Beacon programs in New York City under DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: I didn't just learn about Beacon programs, I lived them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: And growing up, I was a participant in Beacon program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: It wasn't just a place to go to.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: For after school, it was a safe space where I could be myself, build relationships, and stay engaged in positive activities during critical hours of the day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: The after school program gave me more than structure, it gave me opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: I had access to academic support that helped me stay on track in school, but also creative outlets where I could explore the arts and discover my interests.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: Whether it was theater, music, or other activities, I was encouraged to try new things and express myself.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: In the evenings, the Neighborhood Center programming extended that support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: As a teenager, I had a place to channel my energy in a positive way, from playing basketball to participating in performances and community events.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: I stayed involved and connected.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: The program kept me engaged, active, and surrounded by mentors and peers who helped shape my future.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: Beacon programs do more than fill time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: They build community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: They create safe space, support environments for young people and families, particularly in neighborhoods where those opportunities might otherwise be limited.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: Now, more than 20 years later, I've come full circle.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: Today, I have the privilege of serving as a co-director of a Beacon program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: I helped lead the same kind of programming that once supported me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: The full circle experience is powerful, and it speaks to the long-term impact of these programs, not just in helping young people succeed in the moment, but in shaping future leaders who give back to their communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: When you invest in Beacon programs, you are investing in safety, in opportunity, and the potential of young people in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: Your supported spaces where youth can learn, grow, and thrive, just like I did.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: I'm living proof of the value of this work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: Good job.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Garcia
utterances.text: Got it.

chapters.summary: Pedro Pez shares how all four siblings in his single-parent household grew up in Beacon programs, calling it a second home. He now works for the same program, describing it as a full-circle moment and urging continued belief in young people.
chapters.title: Pedro Pez testifies on Beacon program's impact on his family and career

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: Good evening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: I am truly honored to have the opportunity to speak today and express my gratitude for the Beacon program, a program that has had a tremendous impact on my life and my family's journey.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: Growing up in a single parent household, I watched my mother work tirelessly to provide for our family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: Like many families, we faced challenges.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: There were many times where resources were limited and opportunities seemed out of reach.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: Despite those hardships, my mother never stopped encouraging us to work hard, stay positive, and make the most of every opportunity that came our way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: As a mother of four, she pushed each of us to our best.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: All four of her kids grew up in Beacon, which speaks to just how important the program was to our family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: Beacon wasn't just a place for us to go after school, it became a second home, a support system and a community that helped shape who we are.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: One of the greatest opportunities I was given was becoming part of Beacon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: While the activities and programs were valuable, some of the most meaningful things I gained were the relationships and connections I built along the way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: I met friends who became family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: And mentors who guided me through some of the most important stages of my life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: Those relationships taught me how to communicate, collaborate, and build genuine connections with others.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: As I grew older, I realized that those lessons I learned here extended far beyond the walls of the program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: We can help prepare me for school, work, and life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: It gave me confidence in myself, taught me responsibility, and encouraged me to believe

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: that I could achieve more than I ever imagined.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: Today I have the privilege and honor of working for the very same program that once supported me as a participant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: It is a true full circle moment to go from receiving guidance and encouragement to now being in a position where I can support young people who are walking a path similar to mine is something I never take for granted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: Every day I take the opportunity to give back to a place that gives so much to me and my family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: There is no greater feeling than being able to help a child discover their potential, build confidence, and create memories that may stay with them for a lifetime just as it can do for me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: The impact you make reaches far beyond what you see each day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: To everyone in this room, I ask that you keep believing in these young people involved in this program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: Encourage them to embrace every opportunity, build meaningful relationships, and believe in their own potential.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: You never know where those experiences may lead them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pedro Pez
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Annie Nelson, policy analyst at Children's Aid, testifies about operating a Bronx Beacon serving over 600 participants. She urges a new RFP developed through provider engagement, with base rates reflecting true operating costs and cost-of-living adjustments.
chapters.title: Annie Nelson of Children's Aid testifies on need for new Beacon RFP and true-cost funding

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: Thank you to Chair Stevens, the Children and Youth Committee, and New York City Council for the opportunity to present testimony today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: My name is Annie Nelson, and I'm a policy analyst at Children's Aid.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: For over 170 years, Children's Aid has worked to ensure that young people face no limits to their aspirations or potential.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: In service of this mission, we operate more than 50 DYCD contracts across New York City, including a Beacon Community Center on a school campus in the Bronx that serves over 600 children, youth, and adults as a year-round community hub.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: As a provider operating multiple youth programs, we see firsthand the value of a comprehensive model like Beacon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: Our site operates late evenings, weekends, and throughout the summer because community need does not end when the school day ends.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: At our Beacon, community members of all ages can participate in sports and arts programming, academic enrichment, workforce development, adult education, financial empowerment services, and community events and partnerships with other local organizations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: Yet despite strong community demand, these programs are reimbursed at lower levels than other after-school and summer initiatives, creating persistent and growing strains on providers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: At the same time, we know contracts have not comprehensively been

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: updated in almost a decade, and many requirements and payment structures no longer reflect operational reality.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: We really appreciate that city leaders like Chair Stevens are considering the future of Beacon and Cornerstone programs, and we see this as an important opportunity to issue a new RFP that aligns funding and program designs with the reality of these community-based centers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: At a high level, we urge the next RFP first to be developed through early and structured engagement with providers and families, including concept papers before release, to ensure program design and fiscal assumptions reflect on the ground realities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: Second, establish base contract rates that reflect the true cost of operating extended hour multi-generational community centers, including staffing, security, facilities, and the full range of services these sites provide.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: This should include dedicated support for programs we know have demonstrated high demand, like adult workforce development initiatives, financial empowerment programming, and food access initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: And finally, include cost of living adjustments in all contracts to stabilize the workforce and sustain service delivery over time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: Without these updates, providers will remain stretched to meet expectations that no longer match funding structures.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Annie Nelson
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify, and we look forward to our continued partnership.

chapters.summary: Kate Connolly of United Neighborhood Houses, representing settlement houses operating one-third of Beacons and half of Cornerstones, testifies on chronic underfunding, mental health staffing gaps, NYCHA infrastructure issues, and the need for stipends and rate increases.
chapters.title: Kate Connolly of United Neighborhood Houses testifies on funding and infrastructure challenges

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: Hello and thank you for the opportunity to testify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: My name is Kate Connolly and I'm a senior policy analyst for United Neighborhood Houses, an umbrella organization that represents settlement houses across New York State.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: Settlement houses within our network operate one-third of all beacon programs and half of all cornerstone programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: I just want to start by saying thank you to the chair for hosting this oversight hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: I think attention to these programs are long overdue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: And despite the benefits of these programs that you've heard all throughout today's hearing, both programs are chronically underfunded and have not seen new RFPs in years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: As DYCD and the city plan for the future of these programs with a new RFP or with other updates, they must meaningfully engage community members, families, and staff early and often to ensure that their needs and priorities are shaping decision-making.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: In the meantime, it's critical that the city invest in our city's community centers by increasing overall funding for cornerstones and beacons to respond to the pressures for minimum wage increases and inflation and to address the changing needs of the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: Alongside increasing overall rates, DYCD must permit intern and counselor in training stipends as an eligible budget expense to strengthen the workforce development opportunities for our youth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: And then while funding increases should be the top priority, there are a lot of other challenges that these community centers face.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: For example, many providers cite growing mental health needs among their communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: At current funding levels, hiring qualified mental health staff is often unrealistic, leaving many providers reliant on referrals to external clinics that have long wait lists.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: Programs should have the flexibility and funding.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: to offer on-site services directly or through partnerships with community-based mental health providers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: And then regarding NITRA infrastructure, as you've detailed today, they face significant challenges due to long-neglected buildings, not only disruptions to services, but

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: slow response times to emergency repairs and they also receive fines from DOH and FDNY for issues already reported to DYCD and NYCHA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: As these programs evolve, agencies must establish clear communication and accountability so providers and participants are not left bearing the costs of delayed repairs, fines, or administrative failures.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: We appreciate that DYCD has already started taking some steps.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: But they need to do more.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: And then, as you heard earlier, beacons and cornerstones are so much more than after-school programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: And as the city, however, and as the city continues to work towards a vision of universal after-school, they need to think about how beacons and cornerstones can play a role in this, and especially serving communities that would benefit from more comprehensive services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kate Connolly
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, I just, the universal thing always stresses me out because I feel like we need to fix so much stuff before we get there.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So, but thank you guys.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Appreciate your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I don't have any questions for this panel.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'll be calling our next panel, Vincent Price, Chris Pena, Leonardo Richards, Vinny Dung.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Oli?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How do you say this?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Cote?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Cote?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Dialis.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Felsnebo.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Please say your name before you speak.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I apologize.

chapters.summary: Vincent Price, with Father Speaks Up, testifies about his ex-partner moving his children to another state in 2020 and the lack of court assistance in reestablishing contact. He seeks advice and resources for reconnecting with his kids.
chapters.title: Vincent Price testifies on struggles to stay in his children's lives after relocation

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vincent Price
utterances.text: Okay, my name is Vincent Price, and I'm here with Father Speaks Up, and we're trying to get,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vincent Price
utterances.text: we're trying to be heard about our values and viewpoints as far as us being fathers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vincent Price
utterances.text: I'm basically here today to see if I could get a chance to be in my kid's life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vincent Price
utterances.text: Their mother picked up and left in 2020 and moved to another state.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vincent Price
utterances.text: And I went to the court to advise them of that, and I did find where she moved to, and I filed paperwork.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vincent Price
utterances.text: But after that, I didn't, that as far as it went, I didn't really get no help from the court.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vincent Price
utterances.text: So I'm here today seeking, hoping I could get some type of advice and counsel what to do next.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Assemblymember Diaz
utterances.text: Yes, I'm finished.

chapters.summary: Leonard Richards describes joint custody struggles, including his ex-wife blocking visitation and forcing police-station pickups. He shares photos and evidence of his active involvement with his children and expresses frustration with the court system.
chapters.title: Leonard Richards testifies on custody and visitation challenges as a father

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: Hello, good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: My name is Leonard Richards.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: I'm here part of the Father Speak Up movement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: And myself, I reached out to Dionez because he's the CEO of the movement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: Because going through joint custody with my kids, well, my ex-wife,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: I went into joint custody in 2017.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: Everything was going good.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: Everything was going smooth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: Then next thing you know, she just snapped on me, made it hard for me to see my kids.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: Then when I was trying to go to one of my visitations, I went there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: She didn't open the door for me to see my kids.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: I called the police.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: The police...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: They said it's nothing they could do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: I had to go back to court.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: So I had to go back through the whole ring around with the court system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: I felt they wasn't hearing me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: And I feel like they want us as a dad to snap and put our hands on the woman, which...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: In my case, I'm not going to do it because I know that's what some of the mothers want.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: Not all mothers are like that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: But with my ex-wife, I know that's what she's like.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: That's what she wants me to do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: And ever since winning the joint custody, I've never argued with her.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: I never threatened her.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: I never...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: scared her, anything like that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: And also, I meant to include, I was picking my kids up from the home.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: Then next thing you know, she snapped and said, oh, she feels unsafe for me coming into the building to pick up the kids.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: I don't go upstairs to the door.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: I just meet in the lobby, text her that I'm here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: She's supposed to come down.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: So then she went back to the court, told him she feels threatened, she feels scared.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: So now for the past three years, I've been picking my kids up from the police station.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: I don't want my kids going through that, but they're going through it now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: I can't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: It's about as much as I can do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: My son recently just graduated college from Auburn University, upstate New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: My daughter, she's 10 years old.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: My kids love me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: They love the ground I walk on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: And the fact that she's trying to paint this narrative that I'm not a good father, a good dad.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: It's like I have proof.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: I have pictures.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: I have everything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: I do everything with my kids.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: When I have the time, when I have my weekends with them, I go get them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: We do things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: We go out of state.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: We just enjoy it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: And I'm just trying to be in my kid's life, and that's about it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonard Richards
utterances.text: I want to thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Chris Pena, a birth doula and EMT, testifies about high youth suicide, pregnancy, and STD rates linked to broken families. He shares his experience as a parentally alienated father and advocates for keeping families together through community programs and birthing centers.
chapters.title: Chris Pena testifies on parental alienation and youth health risks

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chris Pena
utterances.text: My name is Chris Pena.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chris Pena
utterances.text: I am a birth doula and journalist out of CUNY Kingsborough Community College.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chris Pena
utterances.text: I've noticed firsthand as an emergency medical technician why we need these beacons for the youth when I witnessed their high suicide rate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chris Pena
utterances.text: Their high pregnancy rate and their high STD rate due to not having any guidance from broken families.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chris Pena
utterances.text: As a survivor of domestic violence and a parentally alienated father of two for 10 years and 15 birthdays, I began advocating for maternal care and against parental alienation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chris Pena
utterances.text: And to keep families together.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chris Pena
utterances.text: And now CUNY Kingsborough Community College is helping me build my freestanding birthing center.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chris Pena
utterances.text: So in order to get these beacons ready to go, I believe we should keep advocating against parental alienation to better guide these youth the way we do through our annual movements like the Brides March to bring

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chris Pena
utterances.text: femicides rates down and keep guiding these youth because now everybody, all the youth around me, all want birthing centers now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chris Pena
utterances.text: So it works.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chris Pena
utterances.text: And thank you for having me.

chapters.summary: Oli Kotik attempts to testify about micro and nanoplastics exposure and children's health. Chair Stevens interrupts and rules the testimony off-topic for a hearing on Beacon and Cornerstone programs, offering to speak with Kotik afterwards.
chapters.title: Oli Kotik attempts testimony on microplastics; chair rules off-topic

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: Sorry, I just felt my topic is a little different, but yeah, of course.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Stevens and committee members, for the opportunity to speak in front of you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: My name is Oli Kotik.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: I'm a New York City resident, and also I'm a representative of an international civic platform called Alatra, which is registered in the United States as a nonprofit organization.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: And Alatra brings together scientists, experts, professionals from different fields and more than 180 countries.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: Our main goal, we are united by a commitment to advance science, a public awareness, to help build a safe and sustainable future for all of us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: And under our legal framework, we also have an established think tank, Alatra Global Research Center, which advances our mission, our educational and scientific mission.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: Through independent and volunteer-driven research and analysis.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: And today, I would like to draw your attention to an issue that concerns the health and future of New York City's children, the exposure of micro and nanoplastics, which is one of the areas that we work on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: Micro and nanoplastics are tiny particles, tiny plastic...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm sorry, how is this connected to the topic, which is...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: evaluating cornerstones and beacons, and then also...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: I don't know why I was put in this line.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Well, this is the hearing of the topic.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm just trying to get an understanding.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Is it connected to those programs?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: No, I registered to the hearing of youth and children, and I'm going to talk about how microplastics is impacting the health of children.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: I don't know if it's specific to cornerstones and beacons.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: I'm sorry about that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The hearing is specific to cornerstones and beacon programs or some of the different resolutions that we heard, which was one of them, the father initiative and some baby bonds and some other ones around investments.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I felt like something.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so I'm sorry, we have to keep you on topic.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I can speak to you afterwards and we can have a conversation if that's OK.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: OK.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: Yeah, I would love that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Oli Kotik
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Vinny Dong, a high school sophomore, testifies in support of Resolution 32-2026, citing PSAL underfunding and inequitable sports access for small schools. He also describes overcrowded and run-down conditions at his Saturday Night Lights program facility.
chapters.title: Vinny Dong testifies in support of Resolution 32 on youth sports funding

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: My name is Vinny Dong, and I'm a sophomore at Queens High School for the Sciences.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: I'm here to talk about the importance of youth sport and this regards to Resolution 32-2026.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: It is beyond essential in this day of age for youth to be able to participate in a quality sports program, whether that is through public schools or through NYC parks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: I believe it is essential for the city council and the city to advance youth sports.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: As a current student in the New York City public school system, PSAL is consistently underfunded.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: PSAL is the only way, one of the only ways we could get into varsity or junior varsity sports.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: And the way that the organization funds teams,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: in regards to big schools, small schools, where they're located within the boroughs is extremely inequitable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: And often small schools or neighborhoods with smaller student counts are often

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: not able to offer a diverse or any sports teams and that is very inequitable within New York City schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: Now on the other part with NYC Parks and specifically the Saturday Night Light programs, there's a huge disparity in the amount of

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: location and the type of programming offered by this program within New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: For example, I go to the Saturday Night Light program at Al Ortiz Recreational Facility.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: The facility gymnasium is very apparently run down.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: We are very overcrowded and it is very clear that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: immediate action or more programs should be opened up to help this effort.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Stevens thanks the panel, directs staff to connect the fathers who testified with Assemblymember Diaz and legal resources, and acknowledges the youth sports testimony as supporting the resolution on sports betting revenue.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I know we were talking about the, you folks were talking about the fatherhood initiative.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: My staff just had, he came, you can get it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens

utterances.text: And so just how I'll be able to take your information and get our information so we can stay in contact.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Oh, yeah.

chapters.summary: Stevens thanks all in-person testifiers, announces the shift to Zoom testimony, and provides instructions for remote participants to use the raise-hand function to speak.
chapters.title: Stevens transitions to Zoom testimony and announces final call

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you for everyone who came here to share your thoughts and experiences today.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: If there's anyone else left in the room who wish to speak but have not yet had the opportunity to do so, please raise your hand and fill out an appearance card with the Sergeant of Arms in the back of the room.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens

chapters.summary: Amanda Wilkinson, a single mother and healthcare provider from West Harlem, begins testifying about bullying in schools before being redirected by Chair Stevens to stay on topic. She then connects her remarks to the fatherhood initiative and the need for collaborative community support.
chapters.title: Amanda Wilkinson testifies on bullying in schools and single mother advocacy

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: The first Zoom registrant is Amanda Wilkinson.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: Hi, good afternoon everyone and thank you Chair Stevens and the Committee on Children and Youth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: I am a single mother.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: I am a healthcare provider and mandated reporter in the city of New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: I'm here in Harlem, West Harlem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: As a mother and as an advocate for my own child, but also in community,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: I have brought more awareness and I've heard more awareness from other parents who have had the unfortunate experience of immense and intense bullying, physical assault and harm

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: within schools where their children were not safe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: But when these reports were being made.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: With school staff by the students or the parents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: Those reports were ignored.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: They were not addressed in any way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: And when reporting has to go above the school leadership, it becomes a hopeless...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Does this have to do with cornerstones or beacon or any of the resolutions?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Otherwise, we need you to stay on topic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: Understood.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: I'm also an advocate for mothers who can't find resolution in having support for their children, like myself.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: Not being able to afford also legal representation or any advocacy when the fathers are absent and they don't have

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: the ability to receive child support or any services that their children do deserve and need by state law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: So I would love to also support mothers in that way and somehow and with community help, but I would also somehow even like to support the fathers who are taking the initiative too, because it's a community, it's a collaborative community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: There's two sides of it as well, but I'm glad to see that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Your time has expired.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amanda Wilkinson
utterances.text: Thank you.

summary: The Committee on Children and Youth, chaired by Council Member Althea Stevens, held an oversight hearing evaluating DYCD's Beacon and Cornerstone community center programs, along with several state and federal resolutions on youth financial security, fatherhood initiatives, and sports betting revenue.

DYCD officials testified on program operations, enrollment numbers, facility maintenance coordination with NYCHA and DOE, and plans for future RFPs, while council members pressed on funding gaps, staffing models, and long wait lists at popular sites.

Public testimony included advocates and providers calling for increased funding and new RFPs, Assemblymember Diaz on the fatherhood initiative, fathers sharing personal custody struggles, and a student advocating for youth sports funding.
title: Committee on Children and Youth
topic: Oversight - Evaluation of DYCD’s Beacon and Cornerstone Programs
